name: Pizza Margherita
short_description: Thin Neapolitan pizza with San Marzano tomatoes, fior di latte and fresh basil — the mother of all pizzas.
history: Pizza Margherita was created in 1889 by Neapolitan pizzaiolo Raffaele Esposito in honour of Queen Margherita of Savoy, who was visiting Naples. He made three pizzas for the royal family and she most preferred the one topped with tomato, mozzarella and basil — said to represent the colours of the Italian flag. The Associazione Verace Pizza Napoletana (AVPN) now strictly regulates authentic Neapolitan pizza, specifying everything from the flour type to the 485°C wood-fired oven temperature and the 60–90 second cooking time.

ingredients.item: fior di latte or buffalo mozzarella

steps.instruction: Dissolve yeast in water, mix into flour and salt to form a dough. Knead for 10 minutes until smooth and elastic. Rise for 8–24 hours at room temperature.
Divide into 250 g balls and rest for 4 hours. Stretch by hand (never roll) into a thin 30 cm disc.
Spread crushed tomatoes thinly, tear over mozzarella and drizzle with olive oil.
Bake on a very hot pizza stone at maximum oven temperature (250 °C+) for 8–10 minutes, or in a wood-fired oven at 485 °C for 60–90 seconds.
Add fresh basil immediately on removal from oven. Serve at once.

name: Ragù Bolognese
short_description: The slow-cooked meat sauce from Bologna — rich with beef, pork, wine and a touch of milk.
history: The first recorded recipe for what we now call Bolognese was registered with the Bologna Chamber of Commerce in 1982 by the Accademia Italiana della Cucina — an attempt to codify and protect the authentic recipe. However, ragù in Bologna dates back centuries, evolving from a medieval dish of minced meats stewed with onions and root vegetables. The official recipe calls for beef and pork, a little tomato, white wine and whole milk, and should be simmered for a minimum of two hours. Spaghetti Bolognese, as known internationally, is considered heresy in Bologna — tagliatelle is the only acceptable pasta.

steps.instruction: Render pancetta in a heavy pan, then add onion, celery and carrot (the soffritto). Cook 15 minutes until soft.
Add beef and pork. Brown thoroughly, breaking up the meat — about 15 minutes.
Pour in white wine and cook until completely evaporated. Stir in tomato paste.
Add milk and simmer until absorbed. Season and reduce heat to the lowest setting. Cook uncovered for at least 2 hours, stirring occasionally and adding a splash of water if needed.
Cook tagliatelle in well-salted water until al dente. Toss with ragù and serve with Parmigiano.

name: Risotto alla Milanese
short_description: Saffron-gilded risotto from Milan — creamy, golden and finished with a lavish wave of Parmigiano.
history: Risotto alla Milanese owes its golden colour and distinctive flavour to saffron, once brought to Milan along trade routes from the East. According to legend, the dish was first served at a wedding in 1574 when a glassblower's apprentice, known for adding saffron to his paints, added it to the wedding rice as a prank. Guests were initially horrified but then delighted. The dish is considered the defining recipe of Milanese cuisine and is traditionally served alongside osso buco as a classic Lombardy pairing.

steps.instruction: Steep saffron in a small cup of warm stock for 10 minutes.
Soften onion in butter and oil over medium-low heat — do not colour. Add rice and toast for 2 minutes, stirring constantly.
Add white wine and stir until absorbed. Begin adding hot stock a ladleful at a time, stirring constantly and waiting until each addition is absorbed before adding the next.
Halfway through cooking (around 9 minutes) add the saffron stock.
After 18 minutes, remove from heat while still slightly firm. Stir in cold butter and Parmigiano vigorously (mantecatura) until glossy and creamy. Rest 2 minutes and serve.

name: Arancini
short_description: Golden, breadcrumbed fried rice balls filled with ragù and mozzarella — Sicily's beloved street food.
history: Arancini ('little oranges') have been made in Sicily since at least the 10th century, their origins rooted in the Arab occupation of the island, which introduced rice cultivation, saffron and spiced meat to Sicilian cuisine. The rice was originally flavoured with saffron (giving the orange colour) and filled with slow-cooked meat. Different parts of Sicily have fiercely debated the correct shape: Palermo insists on conical arancini, while Catania favours round. In 2009, Sicily began the process of seeking EU PGI status for arancini.

steps.instruction: Cook rice in saffron-infused stock until dry and sticky. Spread out to cool completely.
Brown mince with a little onion, add tomatoes and peas. Simmer 20 minutes to a thick ragù. Cool.
With wet hands, take a cup of rice, flatten in your palm, add a spoonful of ragù and a cube of mozzarella. Enclose and form into a ball or cone.
Dip in beaten egg, then roll in breadcrumbs. Repeat for all.
Deep fry in batches at 180 °C for 4–5 minutes until deep golden. Drain and serve hot.

name: Ribollita
short_description: A thick, hearty Tuscan bread soup built on cannellini beans, cavolo nero and yesterday's stale bread.
history: Ribollita means 'reboiled' in Italian, and the dish was born from peasant thrift — leftover minestrone was thickened with stale bread and reboiled the next day, and even reboiled again the day after, each time becoming richer and more flavoursome. It is inextricably linked to the cucina povera tradition of Tuscany, particularly the Florentine countryside. The cavolo nero (black kale) gives it its defining colour and bittersweet character. The dish is considered to be at its best on the third day of reboiling.

steps.instruction: Gently fry onion, carrot and celery in olive oil for 15 minutes. Add garlic and cook 2 more minutes.
Add tomatoes, beans and stock. Simmer 20 minutes.
Mash roughly a third of the beans against the side of the pot to thicken the soup.
Add cavolo nero and stale bread, pressing the bread into the liquid. Simmer 20 more minutes until thick and porridge-like.
Rest overnight, then reheat (ribollire — reboil) the next day. Drizzle generously with olive oil to serve.

name: Cacio e Pepe
short_description: Rome's three-ingredient pasta masterpiece — spaghetti with Pecorino Romano and ferocious black pepper.
history: Cacio e pepe — 'cheese and pepper' — is one of Rome's oldest pasta dishes, believed to have originated with shepherds in the hills of Lazio who carried with them the non-perishable ingredients of aged Pecorino, dried pasta and whole peppercorns. The dish requires no butter, no cream, and no oil — only the starchy pasta water and cheese form the sauce, a technique that demands precision. In 2023, Cacio e Pepe was listed as one of Rome's most protected culinary symbols, with chefs arguing passionately about whether any addition beyond the three core ingredients constitutes sacrilege.

steps.instruction: Toast crushed peppercorns in a dry, wide pan for 1 minute until fragrant. Add a ladle of pasta water and let it reduce by half.
Cook pasta in lightly salted boiling water (use less salt than usual, as the cheese is very salty). Reserve pasta water before draining.
Add al dente pasta to the pan with the pepper. Toss over medium heat.
Away from the heat, add grated cheeses with a splash of starchy water. Toss vigorously — the cheese should melt into a creamy, glossy sauce coating every strand. Add more water if it clumps.

name: Bistecca alla Fiorentina
short_description: A vast, bone-in T-bone of Chianina beef, seared over oak embers and served gloriously rare.
history: Bistecca alla Fiorentina takes its name from Florence and from the traditional Florentine greeting of 'buon vì!' (good eat). It is made exclusively from the Chianina cattle breed, one of the oldest in the world, raised in the Val di Chiana of Tuscany. The Medici family famously served great quantities of beef at a St Lawrence Day feast in 1565, impressing visiting English traders who shouted 'beef steak!' — some claim this is how the word 'bistecca' entered Italian. The steak must be at least 3 cm thick, and in Florence, ordering it 'well done' is considered an insult.

ingredients.item: Chianina T-bone or porterhouse steak (at least 3 cm thick)
coarse sea salt and black pepper

steps.instruction: Bring the steak to room temperature for at least 1 hour. Season generously with salt and pepper.
Get a charcoal or wood fire blazingly hot. Place steak directly on the grill. Cook 5 minutes without moving.
Flip once only. Cook 5 more minutes for rare. The internal temperature should be 52–54 °C.
Rest upright on the bone for 5 minutes. Drizzle with excellent olive oil and serve with lemon.

name: Pesto alla Genovese
short_description: The original pesto — crushed Ligurian basil, pine nuts, Parmigiano and extra-virgin olive oil.
history: Pesto Genovese originates from Genoa, the capital of Liguria, and its history stretches back to at least the ancient Roman condiment moretum — a crushed paste of herbs, garlic, cheese and oil. The modern pesto was first documented in the mid-19th century. It is protected by PGI status and the World Pesto Championship is held in Genoa every two years, with strict rules: the basil must be Ligurian DOP, the grinding must be done in a marble mortar with a wooden pestle, and no blender is permitted. Trofie or trenette pasta are traditional pairings; never spaghetti.

steps.instruction: Pound garlic with salt in a large marble mortar until a paste. Add pine nuts and pound to a rough paste.
Add basil leaves in batches, grinding with a circular motion (not pounding) to release the oil without bruising.
Work in the cheeses, then drizzle in olive oil, stirring to emulsify. The result should be fragrant, vivid green and creamy.
Cook pasta al dente in well-salted water. Reserve a cup of pasta water, then toss with pesto, loosening with water as needed.

name: Osso Buco alla Milanese
short_description: Slow-braised veal shanks with white wine and gremolata — Milan's magnificent main course.
history: Osso buco — meaning 'bone with a hole' in Milanese dialect — refers to the marrow-filled hollow in the cross-cut veal shank. The dish has been made in Milan since at least the 18th century, though early versions lacked tomatoes (introduced later from the Americas). The modern version was codified in the 19th century and is always served with Risotto alla Milanese, forming one of the great classic pairings in Italian cuisine. The gremolata — lemon zest, garlic and parsley — is the finishing touch that cuts through the richness. Diners traditionally use a small spoon to scoop out the marrow from the bone.

steps.instruction: Tie the veal shanks with string to keep their shape. Season and dredge in flour. Brown all over in butter and oil in a wide casserole.
Remove meat. Cook onion, celery and carrot in the same pan until soft. Add wine and reduce by half.
Add tomatoes and stock. Return shanks, cover and braise at 160 °C for 1.5–2 hours until very tender.
Make gremolata: mix lemon zest, garlic and parsley.
Serve shanks over risotto Milanese, scattered with gremolata.

name: Tiramisù
short_description: Espresso-soaked savoiardi, mascarpone cream and a dusting of cocoa — Italy's most famous dessert.
history: Tiramisù — 'pick me up' in Italian — is a relatively modern creation, invented in the 1960s or 1970s. The most widely accepted origin story points to the restaurant Le Beccherie in Treviso, Veneto, where pastry chef Roberto Linguanotto created it circa 1969. However, several restaurants in Friuli-Venezia Giulia and across northern Italy claim equal credit, and the dish's origins remain hotly contested. Regardless, tiramisù became an international phenomenon with remarkable speed, appearing on restaurant menus globally by the 1980s. Its combination of coffee, cream and alcohol captures the essence of Italian indulgence.

steps.instruction: Beat egg yolks and sugar until pale and thick. Fold in mascarpone until smooth.
Whisk egg whites to stiff peaks and fold gently into the mascarpone mixture in thirds.
Mix espresso and Marsala. Briefly dip each savoiardo (one second per side — not soggy) and lay in a single layer in a dish.
Spread half the cream mixture over the biscuit layer. Repeat with another layer of dipped biscuits and remaining cream.
Dust generously with cocoa. Cover and refrigerate for at least 4 hours, ideally overnight.

name: Cannoli Siciliani
short_description: Crispy fried pastry tubes stuffed with sweetened ricotta and candied fruit — Sicily's most celebrated sweet.
history: Cannoli originated in Sicily during the Arab rule of the island (9th–11th centuries), with some historians tracing them to the Palermo area. The name comes from the cane (canna) tubes originally used for shaping the shells. They were once made only during Carnival season, but their popularity led to year-round production. Authentic cannoli shells are fried in lard and filled to order to maintain crispness — the Sicilian belief is that a cannolo should be filled only at the moment of eating. They were introduced to American culture by Sicilian immigrants, and the phrase 'Leave the gun, take the cannoli' from The Godfather has become iconic.

steps.instruction: Mix flour, sugar, cinnamon, lard, egg and Marsala into a smooth dough. Rest for 30 minutes.
Roll out very thinly and cut into 10 cm circles. Wrap around cannoli tubes and seal edges with egg white.
Deep fry at 180 °C until golden and blistered. Slide off tubes while hot and cool on a rack.
Beat ricotta with icing sugar until smooth. Fold in candied peel and chocolate chips.
Fill shells with ricotta at the last moment using a piping bag. Dust with icing sugar and serve immediately.

name: Spaghetti alla Carbonara
short_description: Guanciale, egg yolks and Pecorino Romano create a silky, intensely savoury Roman pasta — no cream, ever.
history: Carbonara is one of Rome's four iconic pasta sauces, alongside Cacio e Pepe, Amatriciana and Gricia. Its exact origins are debated — one popular theory holds it was popularised after American troops brought bacon and eggs to Rome during World War II; another claims it derives from ancient Roman charcoal workers (carbonari). The first written recipe appeared in 1952. Italian culinary tradition is unambiguous: there is no cream in carbonara. The creaminess comes entirely from emulsifying egg yolk with starchy pasta water and the rendered fat of the guanciale.

steps.instruction: Render guanciale in a cold pan over medium heat until crispy and the fat runs clear. Reserve all the fat.
Whisk egg yolks, whole egg, cheeses and half the pepper into a smooth cream.
Cook pasta in well-salted water to just-al-dente. Reserve at least a cup of starchy water.

Off the heat, toss pasta with guanciale and its fat. Add egg mixture and toss vigorously, adding pasta water a splash at a time until a glossy, coating sauce forms. The eggs must not scramble — heat from pasta alone is sufficient.

name: Focaccia Genovese
short_description: Ligurian flatbread — dimpled, drenched in olive oil and sprinkled with coarse salt, crisp outside and pillowy within.
history: Focaccia Genovese (fugassa in Ligurian dialect) has roots in ancient Phoenician and Greek flatbreads, later adopted by the Romans. It became central to Ligurian culture and is the traditional breakfast of Genoa, eaten warm with cappuccino. Bakeries open before dawn and queues form for the first hot sheet of the day. Genovese focaccia must be at least 2 cm thick and has a dimpled surface formed by pressing fingers deep into the dough before the final rise. An olive oil and brine solution is poured into the dimples before baking. It has PGI protection as Focaccia di Recco (a thinner stuffed version from the town of Recco).

steps.instruction: Mix flour, yeast, honey, salt and 50 ml oil with water. Knead 8 minutes until smooth. Prove 1 hour until doubled.
Oil a large baking tray generously. Stretch dough to fill the tray and rest 20 minutes.
Mix remaining oil with 2 tbsp of water and pour over the dough. Press deep finger-holes all over the surface.
Prove 30 more minutes. Sprinkle with coarse salt and rosemary.
Bake at 220 °C for 20–25 minutes until deeply golden. Eat warm.

name: Panzanella
short_description: Tuscan stale bread salad soaked in tomato juices, dressed with olive oil, basil and red wine vinegar.
history: Panzanella is one of the great cucina povera dishes of Tuscany, originating as a way to use stale, unsalted Tuscan bread (pane sciocco) that would otherwise be wasted. Early versions made before the introduction of tomatoes used onions and herbs. The tomato became central by the 19th century. The dish is associated with summer, when tomatoes are at their peak and heat makes the idea of cooking unbearable. There are lively debates about whether the bread should be soaked or merely moistened, and whether cucumber (controversial) is acceptable.

ingredients.item: stale Tuscan bread, torn into chunks

steps.instruction: Salt the tomatoes, toss and leave in a colander for 20 minutes to draw out the juices. Reserve the juices.
Soak bread in the tomato juices and a splash of water for 10 minutes until moistened but not mushy.
Combine bread, tomatoes, onion and cucumber (if using). Dress with oil and vinegar.
Season and toss gently. Leave to rest 20 minutes so flavours meld. Add basil just before serving.

name: Keilkannan
short_description: A crispy pan-fried cake of mashed potato and cabbage from Aberdeenshire.
history: A traditional Aberdeenshire dish, Keilkannan is a humble combination of mashed potato and cabbage fried until crispy — similar to the Irish colcannon but with a characteristic golden crust.

steps.instruction: Cook the potatoes. Mash and mix with the cabbage and cream. Season with salt and pepper.
Fry the onion until soft and beginning to brown.
Press half the potato and cabbage mixture in an even layer on to the onion and fry for five minutes until well browned and crispy underneath.
Cut the mixture into four quarters and turn them over carefully so that the crispy bit is on top.
Press the remaining potato and cabbage mixture on to the first layer and after a few more minutes, cut and turn again.

name: Arbroath Smokie Toastie
short_description: Flaked Arbroath smokie in a creamy cheese sauce, served on toast.
history: Arbroath smokies are hot-smoked haddock unique to the town of Arbroath in Angus, and hold Protected Geographical Indication status. The smokie toastie is a popular way to enjoy them — the rich, smoky fish folded into a creamy sauce and served on buttered toast.

steps.instruction: Heat the Arbroath Smokie in 150ml of the milk in a saucepan. Bring to the boil, reduce the heat, cover and cook for about five minutes or until the fish flakes easily. Remove the fish and flake.
Mix the flour with the rest of the milk and stir into the saucepan. Bring to the boil and cook for two minutes, stirring continuously until thick.
Stir in the cheese, egg yolk and flaked fish. Season to taste with salt and pepper, then heat through.
Whisk the egg white until stiff and fold in with a metal spoon. Serve on toast.

name: Vegetarian Haggis
short_description: A hearty Ayrshire vegetarian twist on the Scottish classic, with oatmeal, split peas and barley.
history: Haggis is Scotland's national dish, traditionally made with sheep offal. This vegetarian version from Ayrshire replaces the meat with split peas, pearl barley, mushrooms and oatmeal bound together with vegetable stock.

steps.instruction: Soak the split peas in cold water for at least three hours. Boil the barley and drained peas separately for about 45 minutes until softened.
Fry the onions in butter until softened, then add the grated carrot, stirring until both are soft and beginning to brown.
Stir in the mushrooms and spices and fry for a couple of minutes. Add the split peas and oatmeal and stir to coat with butter.
Dissolve the Marmite in the stock and pour 400ml into the pan. Bring to a boil, then simmer until very thick and peas are tender.
Heat the oven to 180C. Stir in the pearl barley, spoon into a greased pudding basin, cover and bake for 30 minutes, then remove the foil and bake for another 30.

name: Rumblethumps
short_description: A Borders bubble-and-squeak of mashed potato, bacon and cabbage fried until golden.
history: Rumblethumps is the Scottish Borders answer to bubble and squeak. The recipe was recorded by Henry Stephenson of Oxnam, Borders, in 1698 — an ancestor of George Stephenson, the famous railway pioneer.

steps.instruction: Boil the potatoes for 15-20 minutes. Meanwhile, fry the bacon in 25g of the butter.
Drain potatoes and peel while still hot. Mash until smooth.
Heat the cream with remaining butter and, when almost boiling, beat into the potato.
Add bacon and chopped cabbage to the potato and mix. Season to taste.

name: Ecclefechan Cake
short_description: A rich tart from Dumfries and Galloway filled with butter, brown sugar, raisins and walnuts.
history: Ecclefechan tart takes its name from the town of Ecclefechan in Dumfries and Galloway, birthplace of the philosopher Thomas Carlyle. The sweet, treacly filling of butter, eggs, dried fruit and nuts in a blind-baked pastry shell is a Scottish classic.

steps.instruction: Prepare a blind-baked sweet pastry 20cm tart shell and leave it in the mould.
Beat the soft butter and brown sugar until well combined. Trickle in the eggs slowly, a little at a time.
Add the cinnamon, lemon juice and zest and mix well. Fold in the raisins and chopped walnuts.
Scoop the mix into the prepared pastry case and smooth out. Bake at 160C for 45 minutes.

name: Dundee Cake
short_description: A rich Scottish fruit cake flavoured with whisky and decorated with concentric rings of blanched almonds.
history: Dundee cake is said to have been created by Keiller's marmalade company in Dundee in the 19th century. It is traditionally made without cherries and decorated with rings of blanched almonds on top.

steps.instruction: Preheat the oven to 150C. Grease and line a 20cm deep cake tin.
Beat butter and sugar until very light and fluffy and add the marmalade. Slowly add the eggs, beating well.
Add the flour, almonds and spices to the batter. Mix slowly, then stir in the mixed dried fruit.
Add the whisky and mix until well combined. Spoon into the cake tin, smooth the surface and arrange the blanched almonds in circles on top.
Bake for two hours until well risen, firm and golden-brown. Leave to cool for ten minutes then remove from the tin.

name: Smoked Mackerel Pots
short_description: Baked mackerel pounded with anchovy and spices, sealed under butter — a Fife potted fish classic.
history: Potted fish was a traditional method of preserving fish in Scotland, particularly popular in coastal communities like those of Fife. The fish is baked, flaked, seasoned with spices and packed into jars sealed with butter.

steps.instruction: Put the filleted mackerel with the bay leaf and a pinch of mace in an oven-proof dish and barely cover with water or stock. Cover with foil and bake at 180C for 20 minutes, then cool slightly.
Lift out the fish and remove the skin and any small bones.
Pound them well, mixing in the anchovy paste, pepper and grated nutmeg to taste.
Put into a jar or pot, pressing firmly and when quite cold pour the melted butter over the top.
Serve cold on toast or yeasted oatcakes.

name: Chicken Tikka Masala
short_description: Glasgow's famous adopted dish — tender chicken in a rich, spiced tomato and cream sauce.
history: Chicken Tikka Masala was invented by Ali Ahmed Aslam at the Shish Mahal restaurant in Glasgow. The story goes that a customer complained his chicken tikka was too dry, so Ali improvised a sauce. It went on to become Britain's most popular curry.

steps.instruction: Heat vegetable oil and butter and add four roughly chopped onions and a pinch of salt. Cook for 20 minutes until soft and golden.
Add the spices and two chopped red peppers, then cook for five minutes more.
Dice the chicken, add and stir well to coat in the paste. Cook for two minutes.
Add chopped tomatoes, tomato puree and 200ml water. Cover and simmer for 15 minutes until chicken is cooked through.
Remove the lid, stir through the mango chutney, double cream and yogurt, then gently warm through. Scatter with coriander and serve with basmati rice and naan.

name: Crappit Heid
short_description: A traditional Hebridean dish of fish heads stuffed with oatmeal, onion and butter.
history: Crappit Heid (Scots for 'stuffed head') is one of the most ancient dishes of the Scottish islands. In the crofting communities of the Hebrides, nothing was wasted — fish heads were stuffed with oatmeal and simmered into a nutritious meal.

steps.instruction: Rub the butter into the oatmeal and the onion. Season with salt and pepper, and bind with milk.
Take a walnut-sized piece of stuffing and wedge it into the cavity underneath the eyes of each fish head.
Place the fish heads in the stew pan so the mouths are facing up. Pour over enough water to just cover the base of the pot.
Put the lid on and simmer gently over a medium heat for 30 minutes.

name: Partan Bree
short_description: A velvety Highland crab soup enriched with orange zest, fennel and cream.
history: Partan Bree (Scots Gaelic for 'crab broth') is a classic Highland soup that showcases the fine seafood of Scotland's west coast. Historically made with freshly caught crab, the dish has been a Highland staple for generations.

steps.instruction: Separate the white and brown crab meat. Fry the onions, fennel, celery, carrot and garlic and sweat until tender.
Add the bay leaf, thyme, coriander, tomato puree and stock. Bring to a boil then reduce to a simmer.
Mix in the orange zest and brown crab and simmer for an hour. Add the milk and cook for an additional 10 minutes.
Allow to cool slightly and mash. Season the white crab meat separately. Serve with rice.

name: Scotch Pie
short_description: A sturdy hot-water crust pastry pie filled with seasoned mutton mince — the unofficial pie of Scotland.
history: The Scotch pie is a double-crust mutton pie with a distinctive raised hot-water pastry casing. It has been a staple of Scottish football grounds and bakeries since the 19th century, and Lanarkshire has a strong tradition of pie-making.

steps.instruction: Preheat oven to 200C. Mix all filling ingredients and season generously. Divide into four balls. Refrigerate.
Heat 160ml water, salt and lard until boiling. Pour over flour and mix. Knead until smooth when cool enough.
Cut off a quarter of the pastry for lids. Divide the rest into four and roll to 18cm circles. Gather pastry around each meat ball, bringing sides 2cm above the meat.
Dampen edges and press lids on top. Wrap strips of greaseproof paper around each pie and secure with string.
Cut a steam hole in each, brush with beaten egg and rest in the fridge for 30 minutes. Bake for 40 minutes until golden-brown.

name: Cock-a-Leekie
short_description: Scotland's national soup — chicken slow-cooked with leeks, prunes and herbs.
history: Cock-a-Leekie is one of Scotland's oldest soups, first recorded in the 16th century. It was historically made with an old cockerel simmered for hours with leeks. Edinburgh and the Lothians were at the heart of Scottish culinary culture.

steps.instruction: Preheat the oven to 200C. Roast the chicken pieces for around 30 minutes then place in a heavy-bottomed pot along with any juices.
Pour water over the chicken until covered. Bring to the boil and simmer for an hour.
Add the vegetables, prunes, herbs, pepper and salt. Cook until the vegetables are tender.
Remove the chicken, take the meat from the bones and stir back into the soup. Discard bones and herbs, check seasoning and serve.

name: Bere Bread
short_description: A griddle-cooked flatbread made from bere — an ancient barley variety grown in Orkney for millennia.
history: Bere is one of Britain's oldest cereal crops, a six-row barley grown in Orkney since Viking times. The Marwick family, tenant farmers on the Breckness Estate from 1714, grew bere each year. Thomas Marwick emigrated to South Africa in 1862, but the tradition lives on in Orkney.

steps.instruction: Mix together the dry ingredients in a large bowl. Make a well in the centre and add enough buttermilk to make a stiff dough.
Roll out on a floured surface to form one large bannock, or cut into quarters.
Heat a dry pan until moderately hot. Cook the bannocks for about five minutes on each side, and a few minutes on the edges to seal.

name: Bara Brith
short_description: Welsh speckled bread — dried fruit soaked in tea overnight, then baked into a moist loaf.
history: Bara Brith means 'speckled bread' in Welsh. It is one of Wales's most beloved baked goods, traditionally made on bread-baking day using leftover dough mixed with dried fruit and spices. The tea-soaking method keeps the fruit moist and the loaf rich.

steps.instruction: In a large bowl soak the fruit and sugar in strained tea and leave overnight.
Next day preheat the oven to 170C. Line a 900g loaf tin with baking parchment.
Mix the remaining ingredients into the fruit mixture and beat well.
Pour into the loaf tin and bake for 1.5 hours or until a skewer inserted into the middle comes out clean.

name: Sgadan Abergwaun
short_description: Fishguard herrings layered with potato, apple and onion, baked in a mustard and sage sauce.
history: Sgadan Abergwaun (Fishguard Herring) is a traditional Dyfed dish named after the town of Abergwaun (Fishguard) on the Pembrokeshire coast. Herring was the staple fish of Welsh coastal communities for centuries.

steps.instruction: Lightly grease a dish and line with half the sliced potatoes, then layer with sliced apple, then onion and finally the herring.
Paint with mustard and sprinkle with chopped sage, salt and pepper.
Cover with the remaining potatoes and half fill the dish with boiling water. Put the rest of the butter on top, cover and bake at 200C for 45 minutes.
Remove the lid and allow the top to brown for a further half an hour.

name: Baked Stuffed Mackerel
short_description: Whole mackerel stuffed with a herby breadcrumb filling and baked until golden.
history: Gwent (now Monmouthshire) borders England and has a strong tradition of using the plentiful mackerel from the Bristol Channel. This baked stuffed mackerel is a classic example of Welsh coastal cooking.

steps.instruction: Preheat the oven to 200C. Rinse the mackerel and pat dry. Season with salt and pepper.
Mix the breadcrumbs with the herbs, pepper and butter and just enough hot water to make the mix come together.
Cover a baking tray with foil. Spread stuffing on top of foil. Place fillets on top of stuffing.
Brush fillets with remaining butter and sprinkle with onions. Top with dill and cover the tray tightly with another piece of foil.
Bake at 200C for 60 minutes.

name: Cregyn Gleison
short_description: Mussels from Gwynedd cooked in sherry with mushrooms, breadcrumbs and a cream sauce.
history: Cregyn Gleison (blue shells / mussels in Welsh) is a traditional Gwynedd dish showcasing the fine mussels from the Menai Strait and Cardigan Bay coastline. The Battcock family spent many Easters at Nant Caw in the Dysynni Valley in Gwynedd.

steps.instruction: Wash and scrub the mussels. Cook in water until shells open. Remove mussels from shells, strain and reserve the liquid.
Fry finely chopped onions, garlic and mushrooms in butter.
Add the breadcrumbs, lemon juice, mussel liquor, parsley, sherry and season. Bring just to boiling point and simmer gently for about five minutes.
Finally add the egg yolk and cream beaten together. Stir well and add the mussels. Heat up but do not let the sauce boil once the egg yolk and cream are added.

name: Pastai Goci
short_description: A South Glamorgan cockle pie with a shortcrust pastry case and cockle and stock filling.
history: Cockles have been harvested from the Gower and Swansea Bay coastlines since Roman times. The Penclawdd cockle beds in South Glamorgan supplied cockles to markets across South Wales. This traditional cockle pie is a celebration of that coastal heritage.

steps.instruction: Scrub the shells well to remove sand and grit. Cook in a little water and remove from the shells. Keep the cockle liquor after straining.
Take a deep pie dish and line with thickly rolled short crust pastry.
Place a layer of cockles on the bottom, a layer of chopped chives, then repeat. Pour over the reserved cockle stock.
Brush over with milk and bake in a hot oven 200C for about 30 minutes or until the pastry is golden.

name: Glamorgan Sausage
short_description: Crispy Welsh Cheddar and leek vegetarian sausages — veggie before it was fashionable.
history: Glamorgan sausages are entirely meatless, made from Welsh Cheddar, leeks and breadcrumbs — predating Linda McCartney's range by over a century. First recorded in the 19th century, they were a practical solution when meat was expensive. They remain a Welsh classic.

steps.instruction: Fry the leeks in butter until very soft but not browned.
Put 100g of the breadcrumbs, the parsley, thyme and cheese in a large mixing bowl and mix. Beat the egg yolks with the mustard, salt and pepper.
Tip the leeks into the bowl, add the egg yolks and mix well. Divide into eight portions and roll into sausage shapes. Chill on a lined tray.
Whisk the egg whites until just frothy. Dip sausages into egg white and roll in remaining breadcrumbs. Chill for 30 minutes.
Fry in oil over a medium heat, turning regularly until golden-brown and crisp.

name: Nyth Cennin
short_description: A Powys leek and potato nest filled with hard-boiled eggs in a rich cheese sauce.
history: Nyth Cennin means 'leek nest' in Welsh. This hearty dish from Powys, the largest and most rural county in Wales, turns the humble leek into a centrepiece, mounding creamy leek mash around hard-boiled eggs glazed with a Welsh cheese sauce.

steps.instruction: Hard boil the eggs. Boil potatoes and mash.
Clean the leeks and chop small. Cook in boiling salted water for 10 minutes. Strain and add to the hot mashed potato with butter and cream. Season.
Spoon the leek mash into a wide shallow dish, keeping it to the edges to form a 'nest'.
Melt butter, stir in flour, then gradually add milk and cream. Add grated cheese to make a thick sauce.
Shell the hard-boiled eggs, chop roughly and place in the centre of the dish. Pour the cheese sauce over the eggs, sprinkle with extra cheese and brown under a hot grill.

name: Dulse Champ
short_description: Ulster's seaweed-enriched version of champ — mashed potato with dulse, leeks and butter.
history: Dulse (Rhodymenia palmata) is a reddish-brown seaweed harvested from all the coasts of Ireland. Champ is a beloved Northern Irish dish of mashed potato; this Antrim variation uses dulse for a distinctly coastal flavour.

steps.instruction: Soak the dulse for three hours in cold water, then simmer in milk for 30 minutes with a knob of butter and pepper.
Cook the chopped leeks in the milk; drain but reserve the milk. Cook and mash the potatoes, season to taste.
Add the leeks and dulse to the mash. Beat well together and add enough of the reserved hot milk to make the dish creamy and smooth.
Put into a deep warmed dish, make a well in the centre and pour the hot melted butter into it. Dip the potato into the butter when serving.

name: Cockle Soup
short_description: A creamy County Down soup of cockles and celery in a white sauce.
history: County Down has one of Ireland's finest coastlines, and cockles have been gathered from Strangford Lough and Dundrum Bay for centuries. This simple cockle soup is a traditional home recipe from the area.

steps.instruction: Boil the cockles in water until they open. Strain the liquid and shell the cockles.
Heat the butter and stir in the flour. Gradually add the cockle liquid and the milk, stirring all the while.
Add the celery, salt and pepper. Simmer for 30 minutes.
Add cockles and parsley and simmer a few minutes longer.

name: Derry Duck with Londonderry Potatoes
short_description: Duck breasts in a sticky maple and balsamic glaze, served with crispy golden new potatoes.
history: Derry (Londonderry) is the second-largest city in Northern Ireland. This dish pairs a sweet and sticky glaze of maple syrup, soy and balsamic with duck and golden new potatoes, reflecting the modern culinary creativity of the city.

steps.instruction: Boil the maple syrup, soy sauce and balsamic vinegar in a pan until reduced to a sticky glaze. Add the orange juice and reduce again.
Boil the potatoes until soft but not falling apart. Remove and put in cold water.
Season the duck breasts and fry skin-side down for about 8 minutes until brown. Transfer to the oven and cook for 3 minutes.
Remove from the oven, coat in the glaze, and rest for 10 minutes.
Heat the duck fat and fry the potatoes until golden-brown on all sides. Season and serve alongside the duck.

name: Irish Stew
short_description: A slow-cooked Tyrone stew of shoulder lamb layered with potatoes and onions.
history: Irish stew is one of Ireland's most iconic dishes. Tyrone's version is traditional and honest — shoulder of lamb or mutton slow-cooked with potatoes and onions. Mutton from the hill sheep of Tyrone gave this stew its characteristic depth of flavour.

steps.instruction: Dice the lamb into fairly large pieces. Peel and slice the potatoes and onions.
Put layers of potatoes, meat and onion with seasoning into a large pot, finishing with a layer of potatoes.
Pour the liquid over and bring to the boil. Simmer gently for about two hours or cook in a slow oven at 150C.
Check during cooking, adding more liquid if required.

name: Bedfordshire Clanger
short_description: A pastry roll with savoury pork at one end and sweet apple at the other — a full meal in one.
history: The Bedfordshire Clanger was the working man's portable meal — a long pastry roll with savoury meat at one end and sweet fruit at the other. It was eaten by agricultural labourers and brickworks workers across Bedfordshire.

steps.instruction: Mix the pastry with the salt and butter. Add ice water until the dough just comes together. Refrigerate for at least an hour.
Mix the pork with breadcrumbs, chopped sage, crushed garlic, salt and pepper. Chill.
Mix the apples with brown sugar, lemon juice and spices.
Roll out dough to a 10cm by 25cm rectangle. Spoon apple filling along the bottom right half and pork filling along the bottom left half. Fold over and press to seal. Brush with egg.
Cut slits in the apple end only. Refrigerate for 30 minutes. Bake at 220C for 50-60 minutes until golden and bubbling.

name: Windsor Bean Pudding
short_description: A steamed Berkshire pudding of broad beans, spinach and cheese — a Windsor classic.
history: Berkshire is home to Windsor Castle and a long tradition of market gardening along the Thames valley. Broad beans were a staple crop in the area. Aaron Imblin, an ancestor of the family, was born in 1777 and settled in Reading, Berkshire.

steps.instruction: Cook the broad beans until tender. Add the spinach and mash together.
Add butter, cream, cheese and seasoning. Allow to cool.
Stir while adding the yolk of one egg. Place in a pudding bowl and steam for 40 minutes.

name: Birmingham Balti
short_description: A rich, aromatic lamb curry cooked in a karahi — Birmingham's most famous culinary contribution.
history: The Balti was invented in Birmingham's Sparkbrook district in the 1970s by Pakistani immigrants, and the area became known as the 'Balti Triangle'. Cooked and served in a thin pressed-steel karahi, it is distinct from other curries in its fast cooking method and fresh spices.

steps.instruction: Dice the lamb and mix with yoghurt, marinade spices and a teaspoon of salt. Leave for at least two hours or overnight.
Toast the coriander and cumin seeds in a dry pan, then grind. Add chilli, turmeric, garam masala, cardamom and bay.
Fry grated ginger and garlic for two minutes, then add onion and cook for 15 minutes until soft. Add tomatoes and 500ml boiling water. Simmer 30 minutes.
Stir in methi leaves, tamarind and honey. Brown the lamb then add to the sauce. Cover and cook at 170C for 1 hour 30 minutes until tender.

name: Bristol Hotpot
short_description: A West Country pork and cider hotpot with a crispy potato and artichoke topping.
history: Bristol's hotpot draws on the West Country's great cider-making tradition, using local cider to braise pork shoulder with vegetables and apples. Jerusalem artichokes give the topping an earthy sweetness.

steps.instruction: Pre-heat the oven to 180C. Fry onions and celery in butter. Add the chopped vegetables and cook for 20 minutes.
Add peeled and chopped apples, sage, salt and pepper, then the tomatoes. Add cider and stock. Dice pork and add to the pot. Simmer to thicken.
Peel and thinly slice the potatoes and artichokes and layer on top. Season and bake with lid on for 1.5 hours.
Remove the lid, brush topping with butter or oil and return to oven for 15 minutes to crisp.

name: Buckinghamshire Dumpling
short_description: A steamed suet roll stuffed with bacon, liver and herbs — a Chilterns farmhouse classic.
history: The Bonseys were Huguenot immigrants from France who settled in Buckinghamshire, running butcher's shops in Gerrards Cross and Chalfont St Peters. Olive Mary Bonsey, the family grandmother, was born in the George Inn in Chalfont St Peters in 1901.

steps.instruction: Roll the pastry out to a rectangle about 1cm thick.
Spread the bacon rashers, liver and onions evenly over the pastry, leaving a 10cm border. Sprinkle with chopped herbs and pepper.
Dampen the edges, roll up lightly and seal. Wrap in pleated greaseproof paper then loosely with foil, sealing securely.
Steam for three hours. Serve hot.

name: Cambridge Sauce
short_description: A punchy Cambridgeshire condiment of pounded egg yolks, anchovies, capers and herbs — perfect with cold meats.
history: Cambridge sauce is a Victorian-era British condiment. Its sharp, anchovy-rich flavour suits cold roast beef and poached fish. Humphrey Battcock moved out of London to farm in Cambridgeshire, and the family has connections to the county through Battcock Island on the River Ouse.

steps.instruction: Hard-boil the eggs. Pound together the yolks with the anchovies, capers and herbs into a paste.
Beat well together with the mustard, oil and vinegar. Season with a little cayenne.
Put the sauce in a bowl and stir with a whisk to smooth it. Finish with one teaspoon of chopped parsley.

name: Cheshire Onion Pie
short_description: A Cheshire cheese and onion open tart — sharp, crumbly and deeply savoury.
history: Cheshire is England's oldest named cheese, recorded in the Domesday Book of 1086. This open tart uses the distinctive crumbly, slightly salty cheese in both the pastry and the filling, layered with softened onions.

steps.instruction: Mix flour and salt, rub in the butter, stir in the cheese and add enough water to make a stiff dough. Roll out and line a pie plate.
Cook the onions in the butter without browning them.
Put alternate layers of onions and cheese into the pie, finishing with cheese. Season and sprinkle with Worcestershire sauce between each layer.
Bake at 190C for 30 minutes.

name: Cornish Pasty
short_description: A crimped pastry filled with beef, potato, swede and onion — Cornwall's iconic miners' lunch.
history: The Cornish pasty dates back to the 13th century and was the staple meal of tin and copper miners in Cornwall. The thick crimped crust acted as a handle so miners could eat with dirty hands. In 2011 it was awarded Protected Geographical Indication status — only pasties made in Cornwall to the traditional recipe can be called 'Cornish'.

steps.instruction: Rub the butter and lard into the flour with a pinch of salt, then blend in 6 tablespoons cold water to make a firm dough. Cut into 4 and chill for 20 minutes.
Heat oven to 220C. Dice the meat and vegetables and mix together. Roll out each piece of dough to a round about 23cm across.
Firmly pack a quarter of the filling along the centre of each round. Brush the edge with beaten egg, draw up both sides and pinch together to seal.
Lift onto a non-stick baking tray and brush with remaining egg. Bake for 10 minutes, then lower oven to 180C and cook for 45 minutes more until golden.

name: Herb Pudding
short_description: A Cumbrian Easter pudding of bistort, nettles and barley boiled in muslin — one of Britain's oldest spring dishes.
history: Herb Pudding is one of Britain's most ancient dishes, eaten in the Lake District at Easter when the first spring greens appeared. William Kendall Rose, the family grandfather, was born in Ulverston in Cumberland in 1899.

steps.instruction: Chop all the vegetables and tie in a muslin bag with the barley. Boil for two hours.
Turn into a casserole and season. Add beaten egg and a good dab of butter.
Serve piping hot.

name: Chesterfield Oxtail Soup
short_description: A rich Derbyshire oxtail broth with aromatic herbs, sherry and root vegetables.
history: This bold oxtail soup recipe from Derbyshire calls for prodigious quantities — the book notes it is 'a bit over the top'. John Joseph Burnside Rose, the family great-grandfather, was born in Somercotes in Derbyshire in 1873.

steps.instruction: Brown the oxtail pieces in oil with flour, salt and pepper.
Add stock, whole pepper, allspice, basil, marjoram, onions, carrots and celery. Boil for two to three hours.
Remove the bones and strain through a sieve.
Serve with a wineglass of sherry or Madeira.

name: Devon Scones
short_description: Light, golden scones from Devon — best served warm with clotted cream and jam.
history: Devon is famous for the cream tea, and the debate over whether cream or jam goes on first is fiercely contested with Cornwall. The Battcocks and the Greens holidayed in Parracombe, Devon in 1965.

steps.instruction: Preheat the oven to 220C. Mix the flour and baking powder and rub in the butter until the mixture resembles fine breadcrumbs. Stir in the sugar.
Break the egg into a measuring jug and make up to 150ml with milk. Beat lightly. Add to the bowl and mix to a soft dough. Knead until smooth.
Roll out until 1cm thick, cut into rounds and put on the baking tray. Brush with milk.
Bake for about 10 minutes until risen and golden. Cool on a wire rack.

name: Dorset Apple Cake
short_description: A moist Dorset apple cake with wholemeal flour, muscovado sugar and flaked almonds.
history: Apple orchards have been central to Dorset farming for centuries. Alice Rendall, born in Bridport in 1860, whose father owned Rendall and Combs rope makers, trained as a teacher and eventually moved to Chalfont St Peter.

steps.instruction: Heat the oven to 160C. Mix the flour, baking powder, salt, nutmeg and sugar with the butter and eggs. Beat together for a minute until well combined.
Stir in the chopped apples and spoon into a bread tin. Smooth the top and sprinkle with demerara.
Bake for an hour, then add the almonds and bake for a further 15 minutes until coming away from the tin.
Allow to cool for 10 minutes in the tin then lift onto a rack.

name: Bishop Auckland Cheesecake
short_description: A Tudor-style sweet potato tartlet from County Durham, spiked with rum, currants and lemon.
history: This recipe is described as a Tudor dish, using sweet potato which was popular before the regular potato took hold. Isabella Burnside, born in 1851 in Darlington, County Durham, was part of a renowned travelling family who worked as potters.

steps.instruction: Cook and mash the sweet potatoes and beat in the butter, sugar, egg and lemon peel.
Fold in the currants. Line eight tartlet pans with the rolled pastry.
Fill with the potato mixture and bake at 180C for 30 minutes until lightly browned.
When cooked, sprinkle a few drops of rum on to each tartlet.

name: Newent Apple Cobs
short_description: Whole cooking apples stuffed with spiced sugar and wrapped in shortcrust pastry — a Gloucestershire orchard treat.
history: Gloucestershire is famous for its orchards and cider apples. Newent, in the Forest of Dean area, is the centre of the county's apple-growing heritage. Apple cobs — whole apples baked in pastry — have been made in these orchards for centuries.

steps.instruction: Divide the pastry into two portions. Roll each into a square large enough to cover an apple.
Peel and core the apples and set in the centre of each pastry square. Mix the sugar, lemon peel and cinnamon and put into each apple. Add a knob of butter on top.
Bring the pastry up around the apple and seal the edges with water. Place seam-side down on a lined baking tray.
Bake at 160C for 50 minutes until apples are soft and pastry golden. Dust with caster sugar and serve with cream or custard.

name: Hampshire Meatloaf
short_description: A Hampshire steamed meatloaf with mince, soaked bread and shallots — served with brown sauce.
history: Anne Emblin, born in Ardington around 1840, was part of the Imblin/Emblin family who came from Germany and settled in Hampshire. This practical meatloaf using bread soaked in gravy is fine Hampshire farmhouse cooking.

steps.instruction: Mix meat with three onions or shallots. Make 600ml of gravy with stock.
Soak the inside of a small brown loaf with the gravy, beat it well, mix with the mince and beat again. Season and beat in 2 eggs.
Put the mixture in a greased pudding basin and cover with greaseproof paper tied down with string.
Bake for 30 to 40 minutes at 200C. Serve with brown sauce.

name: Herefordshire Curd Pie
short_description: A delicate Herefordshire tart of fresh milk curds, currants, brandy and lemon in a shortcrust case.
history: Herefordshire is prime dairy country. This curd tart is a traditional dessert using freshly made curds enriched with egg, butter, currants and a splash of brandy.

steps.instruction: Curdle the milk by gently heating and adding a tablespoon of vinegar. Strain the curds from whey.
Mix the curd with the butter. Beat an egg and fold into the butter and curd. Add all other filling ingredients.
Make the pastry and line a large deep pie tray. Put in the mixture. Grate nutmeg and add a few shavings of butter on top.
Cook for 30 to 40 minutes at 200C.

name: Hasty Batter
short_description: A quick Hertfordshire baked batter pudding infused with bay leaf and lemon rind.
history: Hertfordshire, just north of London, developed many quick, economical dishes. 'Hasty' puddings were made when time was short. St Albans, the county town, is one of England's oldest settlements with a Roman heritage.

steps.instruction: Mix milk, bay leaves and the rind of a lemon and heat. Add flour until a thick pudding mixture.
Stir in butter, sugar and the eggs.
Whip all lightly together and bake in a deep pastry-lined dish for 20 minutes at 230C.

name: Hull Patty
short_description: Mashed potato and sage patties battered and fried — Hull's own street food.
history: Hull (Kingston upon Hull) has a proud working-class food culture built around its days as one of England's busiest fishing ports. The Hull Patty — mashed potato seasoned with sage and onion, dipped in batter and fried — is sold from chip shops across Humberside.

steps.instruction: Cook and mash the potatoes. Add seasoning and chopped sage and onions. Make patties and put them in the fridge.
Mix the flour and milk to make the batter.
Coat the patties in the batter and fry until golden.

name: Lenten Pie
short_description: A Kentish custard tart in a lard-and-butter pastry, dotted with currants and spiced with mace.
history: Lenten tarts were traditionally eaten during the fasting weeks of Lent, when meat was forbidden. Kent's rich dairy county supplied the milk and cream for these custard tarts, which have been baked across the county since medieval times.

steps.instruction: Rub the fat into the flour, baking powder and salt together. Mix with very little cold water. Roll out thinly, fit to a shallow basin, prick the bottom and bake blind.
Warm the milk, moisten the ground rice with a little warmed milk, and add to the milk. Add spice and bay leaves and cook slowly to the boil, stirring, for 3 minutes.
Remove the bay leaves and stir in the butter and sugar. Allow to cool slightly, then add the lightly beaten egg.
Fill the pastry with the filling, sprinkle over a few washed and dried currants and put in a hot oven to brown.

name: Lancashire Hotpot
short_description: Slow-braised lamb with kidneys and root vegetables under a golden potato lid — Lancashire's iconic dish.
history: Lancashire Hotpot is one of England's most celebrated regional dishes, originating in the cotton mill towns of Lancashire where it could be left to cook slowly all day. The kidney adds depth, and the layered potato lid crisps beautifully in the oven.

steps.instruction: Heat oven to 160C. Dice and brown the lamb and kidneys and set aside. Chop and fry the onions, then add sliced carrots.
Sprinkle over the flour and cook for a couple of minutes. Add Worcestershire sauce and stock, bring to the boil.
Stir in the lamb, kidneys and bay leaves, then turn off the heat.
Slice the potatoes and place in circles on top. Cover, then place in the oven for about two hours.
Remove the lid, brush the potatoes with butter, then brown in a high oven or under the grill.

name: Melton Mowbray Pork Pie
short_description: A hand-raised hot-water crust pork pie filled with seasoned pork and set with savoury jelly.
history: The Melton Mowbray pork pie holds Protected Geographical Indication status. Its uncured meat, hand-raised pastry and natural pork jelly make it distinct from other pork pies. John Rose, born in Ibstock, Leicestershire in 1824, was an engine driver for the Midland Railway Company.

ingredients.item: pork shoulder, minced or finely chopped
pork belly, half minced and half chopped

steps.instruction: Heat the oven to 180C. Mix together all the filling ingredients in a large bowl.
Make the pastry: heat lard and water until lard melts, then stir into flour. Knead when cool enough to handle. Reserve a quarter for the lid.
Press the dough into a 20cm springform tin, building up the sides. Fill with the meat mixture and press down. Add pastry lid, pinch to seal and make a steam hole.
Cook for 30 minutes, reduce heat to 140C and cook for 90 minutes. Brush with beaten egg and bake 20 more minutes. Leave until cold.
Soak gelatine in cold water, squeeze dry and dissolve in hot stock. Cool to room temperature then pour into the pie through the steam hole. Refrigerate overnight to set.

name: Lincoln Salad
short_description: A hearty Lincolnshire salad of lettuce, watercress, beetroot, beef, peas and rice.
history: Lincolnshire is one of England's great farming counties. This robust salad reflects the county's agricultural abundance and is a practical, satisfying dish.

steps.instruction: Wash the lettuce and watercress and tear into small pieces, reserving some whole leaves.
Put everything into a bowl and mix, adding the mayonnaise or French dressing.
Arrange the whole lettuce leaves in a salad bowl, pile the salad upon them and garnish with sliced beetroot.

name: London Particular
short_description: A thick pea and ham soup named after London's notorious 'pea souper' fogs.
history: London Particular is named after the dense yellow fogs that blanketed Victorian London — a mixture of river mist and coal smoke. Thomas William Battcock was baptized in 1781 at St Mary Abbots in Kensington and became a corn and coal merchant at Battcock's Wharf in Chelsea.

steps.instruction: Put a ham hough in a large saucepan with split peas and plenty of water. Simmer very gently for about 30 minutes.
Meanwhile fry the celery, onions and carrots in butter. Add to the split peas.
Add salt and freshly milled pepper, put the lid on and simmer very gently for a further 40-50 minutes.

name: Manchester Tart
short_description: A Manchester classic — raspberry jam and custard cream tart topped with toasted coconut.
history: Manchester Tart is a nostalgic British classic, beloved in school canteens and bakeries across the north-west. Its combination of shortcrust pastry, raspberry jam, custard and toasted coconut has made it an enduring comfort dessert.

steps.instruction: Preheat oven to 200C. Roll out shortcrust pastry to 0.5cm thickness and line a 24cm tart tin. Prick, chill for 30 minutes, blind bake for 15 minutes, then a further 4-5 minutes uncovered.
Spread raspberry jam onto the pastry base. Sprinkle over 3 tablespoons of coconut and half the raspberries.
Bring milk and vanilla to a boil. Beat egg yolks and sugar, pour in the hot milk, whisk. Add cornflour and cook until thick. Cool, then chill 30 minutes.
Whisk whipped cream into the chilled custard. Spoon into the pastry case. Top with remaining raspberries and toasted coconut.

name: Blind Scouse
short_description: A Merseyside vegetable hotpot — Scouse without the lamb, just as warming.
history: Scouse (from 'Lobscouse', a Scandinavian sailor's stew) has been eaten in Liverpool since the 18th century. 'Blind Scouse' is the meatless version, made when lamb was too expensive. Tim, Margaret and Isabel Battcock all studied at Liverpool University.

steps.instruction: Put all ingredients except the butter beans in a large saucepan and bring to the boil.
Cover with a lid and simmer for 30 minutes.
Add the butter beans to the Scouse and return to the boil.

name: Norfolk Pudding
short_description: A steamed suet crust pudding filled with fresh mussels from the Norfolk coast.
history: Norfolk's long coastline has always provided exceptional shellfish. Mussels from the north Norfolk coast are particularly prized. This steamed suet pudding traps the briny, sweet mussels inside a suet crust for a pure taste of the sea.

steps.instruction: Make the suet crust, roll out and line a pudding basin. Wash the mussels.
Cook the mussels in a little water. Remove from their shells.
Put the mussels with a touch of salt and pepper into the pudding basin. Steam for one hour.

name: Hock and Dough Pudding
short_description: A Northamptonshire oven-baked pork hock encased in suet pastry rolls with seasoned minced beef and sage.
history: Northamptonshire was historically a county of shoemakers and farmers. This robust all-in-one dish — a whole pork hock surrounded by suet pastry rolls filled with mince and sage — is hearty Midlands fare.

steps.instruction: Rub the fat into the flour and salt. Add water to make a dough. Roll out and cut into two equal pieces.
Spread the mince and chopped sage onto each piece of dough. Fold over and press the edges together.
Line the sides of the meat tin with these two rolls, put in the hock and surround with sliced potatoes and onions.
Pour over 6 tablespoons of gravy (made from pork trimmings) and cook in a hot oven for about an hour.

name: Pan Hagerty
short_description: A Northumberland pan-fried layered potato, onion and cheese cake — simple and satisfying.
history: Pan Hagerty is a traditional Northumberland dish. Robert Stephenson was born in Wylam in 1803 and worked as a fireman at the colliery pumping engine. His son George Stephenson became the Father of the Railways.

steps.instruction: Grease a pan. Add in layers the potato, onion and cheese, with plenty of salt and pepper, with potato on top and bottom.
Fry the cake until the bottom begins to brown. Turn until all browned on all sides.

name: Cotswold Dumplings
short_description: Crispy fried cheese and egg dumplings rolled in breadcrumbs — a Cotswolds farmhouse nibble.
history: The Cotswolds has been one of England's wealthiest farming regions since the medieval wool trade. These cheese dumplings — bound with eggs and fried in breadcrumbs — are a simple but delicious use of the excellent local cheeses.

steps.instruction: Mix together the cheese, butter and eggs. Add pepper and salt to taste and sufficient white breadcrumbs to make a stiff mixture.
Form into dumplings. Roll in brown breadcrumbs.
Fry in hot fat until brown.

name: Shropshire Pea Soup
short_description: A vivid green Shropshire fresh pea soup enriched with egg yolks and double cream.
history: Shropshire is a pastoral county of rolling hills and market towns on the Welsh border. Fresh peas have been grown here for centuries, and this elegant cream-enriched soup is a classic of English country-house cooking.

steps.instruction: Melt the butter in a large saucepan, add the onion and cook for 5 minutes until soft.
Add the peas, stock and sprigs of mint. Bring to the boil and cook for about 30 minutes. Puree the soup.
Return to the pan and add the seasoning. Beat together the egg yolks and fresh cream and add to the soup.
Heat gently, stirring, but do not boil otherwise it will curdle.

name: Somerset Apple Pie
short_description: A Somerset custard apple tart with brandy and cream, glazed with apricot jam.
history: Somerset is England's great cider county. This elegant tart — apple slices in a custard of cream, egg, sugar and brandy baked in a shortcrust shell — is a celebration of Somerset's finest crop.

steps.instruction: Rub butter into flour until fine crumbs form. Add egg yolk and water. Shape into a disc and chill 20 minutes. Roll out and line a flan tin. Blind bake at 190C for 12-15 minutes.
Reduce oven to 180C. Peel and slice the apples and arrange in the cooled pastry case.
Mix cream with egg, sugar and brandy. Slowly pour evenly over the apples.
Bake for about 35 minutes until custard is just set and apples are golden. Brush top with apricot jam. Unmould and cool.

name: Staffordshire Oatcakes
short_description: Soft, yeasted oatmeal pancakes from the Potteries — a Staffordshire breakfast staple.
history: Staffordshire Oatcakes are unique to the county — soft, yeasted flatbreads made from oatmeal and flour, more like crumpets. The Wedgwood family, who founded the famous pottery dynasty, were ancestors of the family through Jane Wedgwood, born in 1829.

steps.instruction: Mix the water and milk together. Mix the salt to the flour and oatmeal in a large bowl.
Dissolve the yeast with a little warm liquid and add the sugar. Allow the mixture to become frothy.
Mix the dry ingredients with the yeast liquid to make a batter, adding the rest of the warm liquid. Cover and leave in a warm place for about an hour.
Pour enough batter into a pan to make a 22cm oatcake. Flip after 2-3 minutes when the top is dry and the underside is golden brown. Cook for another 2-3 minutes.

name: Suffolk Fish Pie
short_description: A classic Suffolk fish pie of cod or haddock, hard-boiled eggs and parsley sauce under a potato crust.
history: Suffolk's long North Sea coastline has supported a fishing industry for centuries. Lowestoft and Aldeburgh were major fishing ports, and cod and haddock were central to the local diet. This simple, satisfying fish pie is a Suffolk kitchen staple.

steps.instruction: Pre-heat oven to 190C. Cook the fish in milk in a large saucepan, season. Reserve the milk and flake the fish into a buttered deep dish. Boil and slice the hard-boiled eggs.
Melt butter, stir in flour, then slowly add the reserved milk, stirring to prevent lumps. When thickened, add parsley and capers and season.
Pour the sauce over the fish and eggs. Cover with mashed potato, lightly raked with a fork.
Cook for 30 minutes until the potato is lightly browned.

name: Surrey Pudding
short_description: A Surrey steamed suet pudding crammed with mushrooms and onion.
history: Surrey is one of England's most wooded counties, and wild mushrooms have been gathered from its forests for centuries. This steamed suet pudding packed with mushrooms is a Victorian country-house dish perfect for autumn.

steps.instruction: Mix the flour, suet and salt. Bind with cold water into a stiff dough. Roll out and use to line a pudding basin, reserving a little as a lid.
Pack with the uncooked mushrooms, butter and onion.
Cut a lid to fit and seal with water. Steam for 1 to 2 hours.

name: Sussex Pond Pudding
short_description: A whole lemon steamed inside a suet pudding with butter and sugar — the 'pond' appears when you cut it open.
history: John Battcock, born in Storrington, Sussex in 1713, was the village stonemason who rebuilt the church tower of St Mary's in Storrington in 1754. The Battcocks were Sussex stonemasons for centuries, and the county's oldest culinary traditions are well-documented.

steps.instruction: Combine flour, suet, salt and ginger. Add milk and water and stir into a firm dough. Line a greased basin with the pastry, reserving a quarter for the lid.
Mix the butter and sugar and put most in the bottom. Stab the lemon all over and place in the basin. Pack remaining butter and sugar around it.
Roll out the remaining pastry as a lid, moisten the rim and press the lid on firmly.
Steam for about four hours, topping up the water as necessary. Turn out onto a plate — the buttery lemon pond will flow out when cut. Serve with chilled cream.

name: Coventry God Cakes
short_description: Triangular puff pastry pockets filled with a spiced currant mincemeat — a Coventry Easter tradition.
history: Coventry God Cakes are triangular puff pastry cakes filled with spiced mincemeat, traditionally given by godparents to their godchildren at New Year and Easter. The triangle shape represents the Holy Trinity. The family lived in Rugby, Warwickshire from 1990 to 2000.

steps.instruction: Make mincemeat filling by melting butter and stirring in currants, sugar and spices.
Roll out puff pastry about 1cm thick and cut into oblong pieces about 18cm by 22cm. Place a tablespoonful of mincemeat mixture in the centre.
Fold the pastry over to make a triangle shape. Wet the edges and pinch together. Roll lightly to flatten slightly on top.
Make three slashes across the top, brush with egg white, dust with caster sugar and bake in a hot oven suitable for puff pastry.

name: Apple Hearthbread
short_description: A yeasted West Midlands flatbread worked with fried apple slices — rustic and fragrant.
history: The West Midlands has a long orchard tradition, and apples were used in everything from cider to bread. This hearthbread — a flatbread baked on a hot sheet and brushed with olive oil — is a reminder of older British baking traditions.

steps.instruction: Mix flour, yeast and salt. Slowly beat in water, a quarter cup olive oil and honey. Beat for 10 minutes. Cover and let rise in a warm place for 40 minutes.
Slice and fry the apples in butter.
After dough has risen, gently knead in as much flour as needed for a very soft dough. Gently work apple slices into the dough.
Shape dough into a 23cm round on an oiled sheet. Let rise for 30 minutes. Brush with olive oil, sprinkle with coarse salt and bake at 250C for 15-20 minutes until browned.

name: Stir-in Pudding
short_description: A steamed Wiltshire suet pudding with apple, cloves and sugar — Avebury's ancient comfort.
history: Wiltshire is home to Avebury and Stonehenge. This simple steamed pudding — a rubbed-fat pastry stirred together with sliced apple and cloves — is the kind of old-fashioned English comfort food eaten in Wiltshire farmhouses for centuries.

steps.instruction: Rub fat into flour, sugar and salt. Stir in sliced apples with 5 cloves.
Mix with sufficient liquid to make a fairly stiff mixture.
Steam in pudding bowl for two to three hours. Serve with custard and cream.

name: Yorkshire Cabbage Pie
short_description: A Yorkshire baked cabbage and vegetable gratin wrapped in whole cabbage leaves with a cheese and cream custard.
history: The Clark family originally came from Yorkshire — John Clark was born near Ripon in 1808, a wheelwright, carpenter and Methodist preacher who emigrated to Natal, South Africa. This baked cabbage pie is a fine example of the county's inventive vegetable cookery.

steps.instruction: Trim the cabbage and chop half the leaves. Cook the remaining whole cabbage leaves until tender.
Cook the chopped cabbage and other vegetables together until just softened, and coarsely mash.
Butter the inside of an oven dish and line it with the cooked whole cabbage leaves, allowing some to hang over the edge.
Stir the cream, cheese and egg into the mashed vegetables, pour into the cabbage-lined dish, flop the hanging leaves over the top and bake for about 30 minutes at 200C.

name: Alabama Pecan Pie
short_description: A rich Southern pecan pie — corn syrup, brown sugar and a whole heap of Alabama pecans.
history: Alabama is famous for its pecans. Ralph Alexander Gailey, the family great-grandfather, was from nearby Tennessee. Pecan pie became the South's most celebrated dessert, made with the abundant local nuts.

steps.instruction: Preheat oven to 175C. Beat together corn syrup, sugar, eggs, melted butter and vanilla until smooth.
Stir in the pecans. Pour into the unbaked pastry shell.
Bake for 45-50 minutes until filling is set and pastry is golden. The centre should be only slightly wobbly.
Cool completely before slicing. Serve with whipped cream or vanilla ice cream.

name: Alabama Fried Green Tomatoes
short_description: Sliced unripe tomatoes coated in seasoned cornmeal and pan-fried until golden — a Deep South classic.
history: Before tomatoes ripen, Southerners have long fried them in cornmeal — a tradition particularly strong in Alabama. The dish was popularised by the novel Fried Green Tomatoes at the Whistle Stop Cafe, set in Irondale, Alabama.

steps.instruction: Slice tomatoes 1cm thick. Season well with salt and pepper.
Beat eggs with milk in a bowl. Mix cornmeal and flour in a second bowl.
Dip each tomato slice in the egg mixture, then coat thoroughly with the cornmeal mixture.
Fry in hot oil for 2-3 minutes per side until golden and crispy. Drain on paper towels and serve hot.

name: Alaska Salmon Pie
short_description: A hearty Alaskan pie of wild salmon and mashed potato in a golden pastry crust.
history: Alaska's cold, clear waters produce some of the finest wild salmon in the world. This savoury salmon pie is a staple of Alaskan home cooking, making the most of the state's most abundant natural resource.

steps.instruction: Preheat oven to 180C. Line a pie dish with pastry.
Mix salmon, mashed potato, onion, eggs and cream. Season well.
Pour filling into the pastry shell and cover with the pastry lid. Seal edges and cut steam holes.
Bake for 35-40 minutes until golden. Rest 10 minutes before serving.

name: Navajo Frybread Taco
short_description: Puffy golden frybread topped with seasoned beef, lettuce, tomato and cheese — born of necessity, now iconic.
history: Frybread was created in 1864 when the Navajo people were forced on the Long Walk to internment at Bosque Redondo. US government rations of flour, lard and sugar were transformed into frybread. Now celebrated across the Southwest as an Indian Taco.

ingredients.item: ground beef, seasoned with cumin and chilli
shredded lettuce, tomato, cheese and sour cream

steps.instruction: Mix flour, salt and baking powder. Gradually add warm water to form a soft dough. Cover and rest 30 minutes.
Pull dough into rounds about 20cm across. Fry in hot oil for 1-2 minutes per side until puffy and golden.
Brown ground beef with cumin and chilli in a skillet. Drain excess fat.
Top frybread with seasoned beef then lettuce, tomato, cheese and sour cream. Serve immediately.

name: Arkansas Biscuits and Gravy
short_description: Fluffy buttermilk biscuits smothered in a rich, peppery pork sausage gravy.
history: James Hadley, an ancestor of the Mathews family through Allison, rode with General Custer in the Arkansas Expedition during the Civil War. Biscuits and gravy is the quintessential Arkansas breakfast, beloved throughout the South.

ingredients.item: salt and plenty of black pepper

steps.instruction: Preheat oven to 220C. Cut shortening into flour until crumbly. Stir in buttermilk just until the dough comes together.
Drop spoonfuls onto a baking sheet and bake for 12-15 minutes until risen and golden.
Meanwhile, brown sausage in a heavy skillet, breaking it up. Stir in flour and cook for 2 minutes.
Gradually whisk in milk. Simmer, stirring, until thick. Season very generously with salt and black pepper.
Split biscuits open and smother in sausage gravy. Serve immediately.

name: California Baja Fish Tacos
short_description: Beer-battered fish in warm corn tortillas with shredded cabbage and a tangy crema — sun, surf and Baja.
history: Scott Mathews, the family patriarch, grew up in San Francisco where Benjamin and Eleanor Mathews had been prominent civic figures. Fish tacos crossed from Baja California into Southern California in the 1970s and became a California institution.

ingredients.item: sour cream mixed with lime juice

steps.instruction: Make a batter by whisking flour, baking powder and cold beer until smooth. Season with salt.
Cut fish into strips. Dip in batter and fry in hot oil for 2-3 minutes until golden and crispy. Drain.
Warm tortillas. Fill each with fish strips and shredded cabbage.
Drizzle with lime crema and hot sauce. Serve with lime wedges.

name: Colorado Denver Omelette
short_description: A classic Western omelette of eggs, ham, onion and green pepper — the cowboy's breakfast.
history: The Denver omelette originated in Colorado gold rush camps where cowboys and miners needed a hearty, quick breakfast. Ham, onion and green pepper were staples of chuck wagon cooking. Also served as the Denver Sandwich on toast.

steps.instruction: Sauté ham, green pepper and onion in butter over medium heat until softened, about 4 minutes.
Beat eggs with milk, salt and pepper.
Pour eggs over the vegetables. As the edges set, lift them to let uncooked egg run underneath.
When nearly set, fold in half and slide onto a warm plate. Serve with toast and hot sauce.

name: Connecticut Election Cake
short_description: A yeasted spiced fruit cake with dried fruit and brandy — Connecticut's great civic tradition baked for Election Day.
history: Before the Revolution, Election Cake was known as Muster Cake, baked for Training Day when the militia assembled. After independence it was served on Election Day — the yeast-raised fruit cake fuelled democracy in New England for over two centuries.

steps.instruction: Dissolve yeast in warm milk with a teaspoon of sugar. Let stand 10 minutes until frothy.
Beat butter and remaining sugar until light. Beat in eggs, then add flour alternately with the yeast mixture. Stir in spices and brandy.
Fold in raisins and dried fruit. Cover and let rise in a warm place for 2 hours.
Pour into a greased loaf tin and bake at 180C for 45-50 minutes until a skewer comes out clean.

name: Delaware Scrapple
short_description: A pan-fried slab of pork and cornmeal loaf — Delaware's German-heritage breakfast staple.
history: David Reed, a family ancestor, fought with the Delaware Blue Hens regiment in the Revolutionary War. Scrapple is Delaware's most distinctive food — a German-influenced pork and cornmeal loaf introduced by Pennsylvania German settlers, sliced and fried until crispy.

steps.instruction: Simmer pork in 1 litre of water for 2 hours. Remove meat, shred finely and return to strained broth.
Bring broth back to a boil. Gradually whisk in cornmeal, flour and all seasonings.
Cook, stirring constantly, until very thick. Pour into a greased loaf pan and refrigerate overnight.
Slice about 1cm thick and fry in butter over medium-high heat until golden and crispy on each side. Serve with maple syrup.

name: Florida Jezebel Sauce
short_description: A fiery-sweet condiment of apple jelly, pineapple jam and horseradish — served with cream cheese and crackers.
history: Jezebel Sauce became a Southern party staple in the 1950s — named for the biblical queen's beguiling combination of sweetness and heat. In Florida it is poured over cream cheese as an appetiser, or used as a glaze for pork and chicken.

steps.instruction: Combine apple jelly, pineapple jam, horseradish, mustard powder and pepper in a bowl. Stir until completely blended.
Taste and adjust — add more horseradish for heat, more jelly for sweetness.
Pour into a jar and refrigerate. The sauce improves over a few days and keeps for months.
To serve, pour generously over a block of cream cheese and surround with crackers.

name: Georgia Peach Cobbler
short_description: Juicy Georgia peaches baked under a golden self-rising batter — the South's most beloved summer dessert.
history: Mike and Allison Battcock made a road trip through Georgia in 2018, driving through Atlanta. Georgia is the Peach State — the fruit has been central to its identity since the 19th century. Peach cobbler is the state's most beloved dessert.

steps.instruction: Preheat oven to 180C. Melt butter in a 23x33cm baking dish in the oven.
Toss peaches with half the sugar. Whisk flour, remaining sugar, baking powder, milk and cinnamon into a smooth batter.
Pour batter over the melted butter — do not stir. Spoon peaches and their juice on top — do not stir.
Bake for 45 minutes until top is golden and a crust has formed. Serve warm with vanilla ice cream.

name: Guam Eskebeche
short_description: Chamorro pickled fish in vinegar, turmeric and onion — a living link to Guam's Spanish colonial heritage.
history: Guam's indigenous Chamorro people have cooked fish in this pickled style since Spanish colonial rule began in 1668. Eskebeche (from the Spanish escabeche) is the island's treasured method of preserving fish — a taste of the Pacific and Mediterranean meeting in the South Pacific.

ingredients.item: whole fish or fillets (grouper or snapper)

steps.instruction: Season fish with salt and fry in hot oil until golden on each side. Set aside on a plate.
In the same pan, sauté onion and garlic until softened. Add turmeric and pepper and cook 1 minute.
Pour in vinegar and water. Bring to a boil and simmer for 5 minutes.
Return fish to the pan and simmer in the pickling sauce for 10 minutes. Serve over steamed white rice.

name: Hawaii Poke
short_description: Cubed sushi-grade ahi tuna marinated in soy, sesame and ginger — Hawaii's iconic raw fish bowl.
history: Mike and Allison Battcock visited Hawaii with their family, and poke was their favourite Hawaiian dish. The word poke means 'to cut crosswise' in Hawaiian. What began as a Native Hawaiian tradition of seasoning reef fish scraps is now celebrated worldwide.

steps.instruction: Cut tuna into 2cm cubes with a very sharp knife.
Whisk together soy sauce, sesame oil and grated ginger.
Toss tuna gently with the dressing. Fold in spring onions and avocado. Refrigerate for 15 minutes.
Serve over a bowl of sushi rice, sprinkled with sesame seeds. Add sriracha if you like heat.

name: Idaho Funeral Potatoes
short_description: A creamy, cheesy potato casserole with a buttery cornflake topping — the quintessential Mormon comfort dish.
history: It had to be potatoes for Idaho — the state supplies a third of all US potatoes. Funeral Potatoes get their name from being the casserole brought to bereaved families across the Mormon West. They became so iconic that the 2002 Salt Lake City Winter Olympics featured them on a commemorative pin.

steps.instruction: Preheat oven to 180C. Mix hash browns, soup, sour cream, cheese, half the butter, onion and seasoning until combined.
Spread into a greased 23x33cm baking dish.
Mix crushed cornflakes with remaining melted butter. Sprinkle evenly over the top.
Bake uncovered for 45-50 minutes until golden and bubbling throughout.

name: Chicago Deep Dish Pizza
short_description: A buttery, deep-dish pizza with layers of mozzarella, sausage and chunky tomato sauce baked in a tall pan.
