history: Gabriel Battcock spent a year at the University of Illinois. Benjamin Canby Mathews of the family fought in the Civil War with Illinois regiments. Chicago deep dish pizza was invented at Pizzeria Uno in 1943 by Ike Sewell — a revelation in which the sauce goes on top.

steps.instruction: Mix flour, semolina, yeast and salt. Combine water and oil. Mix to form a dough. Knead 5 minutes and let rise 1 hour.
Press dough into a well-oiled 30cm deep-dish pan, pulling it up the sides at least 4cm.
Layer mozzarella on the base, then sausage. Season crushed tomatoes and spoon on top as the topmost layer.
Bake at 220C for 25-35 minutes until the crust is deep golden and filling is bubbling.
Rest for 5 minutes before slicing into wedges.

name: Indiana Sugar Cream Pie
short_description: Indiana's official state pie — a simple, silky custard of cream, sugar and nutmeg in a pastry shell.
history: Sugar Cream Pie, sometimes called Hoosier Pie, was the practical dessert of Indiana's Quaker settlers. Made with just cream, sugar and flour — no eggs — it was born of necessity and became the state's signature dish. Indiana is the only state with an official sugar cream pie.

steps.instruction: Preheat oven to 190C.
Whisk together cream, sugar, flour, melted butter and vanilla until smooth.
Pour into the unbaked pastry shell. Grate nutmeg generously over the surface.
Bake for 45-50 minutes until the filling is set at the edges but still slightly wobbly in the centre. Cool completely before slicing.

name: Iowa Burgo
short_description: A Midwestern sirloin steak basted in herb butter — simple, bold and deeply satisfying.
history: Allison Battcock went to university at Grinnell College, Iowa. The Garman family, ancestors through the Mathews line, settled in Iowa in the 19th century. Burgo is a rich midwestern beef dish — straightforward and satisfying, reflecting the agricultural abundance of the Corn Belt.

steps.instruction: Season steaks generously with salt and black pepper. Rest at room temperature for 30 minutes.
Melt butter with garlic, thyme, parsley and Worcestershire sauce in a small pan. Keep warm.
Grill or pan-sear steaks over high heat to preferred doneness, basting with the herb butter throughout.
Rest for 5 minutes. Serve with remaining herb butter poured over.

name: Kansas Cheesy Corn Casserole
short_description: A rich, golden-topped casserole of creamed corn, cheese and butter — a Kansas church supper staple.
history: Ralph Alexander Gailey was born in Kansas in the late 19th century, a pioneer family on the Great Plains. Kansas is America's wheat and corn country. This simple, richly satisfying casserole has fed families at church suppers and harvest dinners across the state for generations.

steps.instruction: Preheat oven to 180C. Mix all ingredients together until combined.
Pour into a greased baking dish.
Bake for 35-40 minutes until set and golden on top.
Allow to cool for 5 minutes before scooping and serving.

name: Kentucky Hot Brown
short_description: An open-faced turkey sandwich drowned in Mornay sauce and finished under the grill — Louisville's most famous dish.
history: The Mathews family have deep roots in Kentucky, said to have been among the first settlers alongside Daniel Boone. Samuel Buchanan of Bourbon, Kentucky is a family ancestor. The Hot Brown was invented at the Brown Hotel in Louisville in 1926 as a late-night supper for dancing guests.

steps.instruction: Make Mornay sauce: melt butter, whisk in flour and cook 2 minutes. Gradually whisk in milk. Add half the cheese. Temper in beaten egg and season.
Place toast in oven-proof dishes and layer with turkey slices.
Ladle Mornay sauce over the top to completely cover the turkey.
Arrange two bacon rashers in an X over each portion. Sprinkle with remaining cheese and paprika.
Grill until sauce is bubbling and beginning to brown. Serve immediately in the dish.

name: Louisiana Shrimp Creole
short_description: Gulf shrimp in a rich Creole tomato sauce built on the holy trinity of onion, celery and green pepper.
history: Gabriel Battcock was in New Orleans in 2013, experiencing the city's extraordinary food culture. Louisiana Creole cuisine fuses French, African, Spanish and Native American traditions. Shrimp Creole is the great Creole classic — a dish as old as New Orleans itself.

steps.instruction: Heat oil in a heavy pot. Sauté onion, celery and green pepper until soft — about 8 minutes.
Add garlic and cook 1 minute. Add tomatoes, tomato sauce, Worcestershire, bay leaves and seasonings.
Simmer uncovered for 20 minutes until sauce thickens. Adjust seasoning.
Add shrimp and cook 3-4 minutes until pink and just cooked through. Remove bay leaves.
Serve over white rice.

name: Maine Lobster Stew
short_description: A rich, creamy stew of Maine lobster in warm milk and cream — the ocean in a bowl.
history: Allison Battcock and her family holidayed in Maine. Maine lobster is world famous — the cold, clear Atlantic waters of the Gulf of Maine produce some of the finest crustaceans on earth. This simple stew is the purest celebration of them.

ingredients.item: lobsters, meat removed and roughly chopped

steps.instruction: Gently sauté onion in butter until softened but not coloured.
Add lobster meat and cook gently for 2 minutes.
Warm milk and cream separately until just simmering. Pour over the lobster.
Season with salt, pepper and paprika. Simmer very gently for 15 minutes — do not boil or the cream will split.
Ladle into warm bowls. Garnish with chives and a dot of butter.

name: Maryland Crab Cakes
short_description: Chesapeake Bay lump crab cakes with minimal filler — all crab, seared golden in a pan.
history: Maryland's blue crab from Chesapeake Bay is one of the most celebrated seafood ingredients in America, harvested since pre-colonial times. True Maryland crab cakes use minimal filler — they are almost entirely sweet, fresh lump crab.

steps.instruction: Pick through crab meat and remove any shell fragments. Handle gently to keep lumps intact.
Mix mayo, egg, mustard, Worcestershire and Old Bay. Fold gently into crab with breadcrumbs — do not overmix.
Shape into 8 cakes and refrigerate for 30 minutes to firm up.
Pan-fry in oil over medium-high heat for 3-4 minutes per side until deep golden. Serve with tartar sauce and lemon.

name: Boston Baked Beans
short_description: Navy beans slow-baked with salt pork, molasses and brown sugar — Puritan Boston in a pot.
history: Jonas Spaulding, a family ancestor, fired one of the first bullets at Bunker Hill in 1775. Boston Baked Beans were the Puritan dish of the Sabbath — cooked all day Saturday in the community oven to avoid working on Sunday. Boston was once known as Beantown.

steps.instruction: Parboil soaked beans for 10 minutes, drain.
Score the salt pork and place it with the onion in the bottom of a heavy casserole or bean pot.
Mix molasses, sugar, mustard, salt and pepper with 500ml water. Add beans and pour mixture over.
Cover and bake at 150C for 6-8 hours, adding water as needed to keep beans just submerged. Uncover for the last hour to reduce.

name: Michigan Red Top Pizza
short_description: Detroit-style square pizza where the sauce goes on top of the cheese, caramelized in a seasoned pan.
history: Gabriel Battcock spent time in Detroit, Michigan. The Detroit square pizza style was invented at Buddy's Restaurant in 1946. The defining feature is the sauce on top of the cheese — the 'red top' — and cheese caramelized against the sides of a well-oiled pan.

ingredients.item: tin crushed tomatoes seasoned with oregano and garlic

steps.instruction: Mix flour, yeast and salt. Add water and oil. Knead 5 minutes. Let rise 1 hour in an oiled bowl.
Press dough into a well-oiled rectangular pan, going up the sides. Let rise 30 minutes.
Press both cheeses over the dough, right to the very edges (so cheese caramelizes against the pan). Add any toppings.
Spoon the seasoned tomato sauce in stripes or dollops on top over the cheese.
Bake at 250C for 15-20 minutes until the edges are caramelized and the crust is deep golden.

name: Minnesota Hot Dish
short_description: Minnesota's beloved casserole — ground beef and vegetables in cream of mushroom soup, blanketed with tater tots.
history: Allison Battcock's family has connections to Minnesota, and the Sykora family history includes Midwestern roots. Minnesota's Hot Dish is the state's comfort food — a casserole that has nourished families through bitter winters since the 1950s. Tater tots on top are non-negotiable.

steps.instruction: Preheat oven to 180C. Brown ground beef with onion, breaking up the meat. Season and drain fat.
Stir in cream of mushroom soup and stock. Add frozen vegetables and stir to combine.
Pour into a large casserole dish.
Cover the entire surface with frozen tater tots in a single layer.
Bake uncovered for 45-50 minutes until tater tots are golden and crispy and casserole is bubbling.

name: Mississippi Mud Pie
short_description: A fudgy chocolate brownie cake topped with molten marshmallows and chocolate glaze — as rich as the Mississippi mud.
history: Mississippi was the site of the pivotal Vicksburg battle in 1863 during the Civil War. This extraordinarily rich chocolate cake — named for its resemblance to the thick Mississippi river mud — is the state's most celebrated dessert, beloved at church picnics and family reunions.

steps.instruction: Preheat oven to 180C. Melt butter and chocolate together. Cool slightly.
Beat in eggs, sugar and vanilla. Fold in flour and pecans.
Pour into a greased 23x33cm pan. Bake for 25 minutes.
Immediately scatter marshmallows over the hot cake. Return to oven for 3 minutes until melted.
Mix icing sugar, cocoa and milk to a thick glaze. Drizzle over the marshmallows. Cool before cutting.

name: St Louis Gooey Butter Cake
short_description: A St Louis accident turned legend — a rich butter-and-cream-cheese filling on a yeasted cake base.
history: Gooey Butter Cake was accidentally invented in a St Louis bakery in the 1930s when a baker inverted the proportions of butter and flour. The mistake was too delicious to correct, and it became the city's signature dessert. The Mathews family had connections in St Louis.

steps.instruction: Dissolve yeast in warm milk. Mix with flour, butter, sugar and egg to form a soft dough. Press into the base of a greased 23x33cm pan. Let rise 30 minutes.
Beat cream cheese and butter together until smooth.
Beat in eggs and vanilla. Add icing sugar and beat until smooth and creamy.
Pour topping over the risen dough base.
Bake at 180C for 35-40 minutes. The centre should still be gooey — it sets as it cools. Dust with icing sugar.

name: Montana Hash Brown Casserole
short_description: A hearty Montana ranch breakfast casserole of hash browns, ham, cheddar and cream of chicken soup.
history: Montana's vast cattle ranches and frontier spirit call for hearty, no-nonsense cooking. This hash brown casserole — the ultimate cowboy comfort food — is served everywhere from ranch breakfasts to church suppers across Big Sky Country.

steps.instruction: Preheat oven to 180C. Combine all ingredients, reserving a handful of cheese for the top.
Spread into a greased 23x33cm baking dish.
Top with reserved cheese.
Bake for 45 minutes until golden and bubbling throughout.

name: Nebraska Runza
short_description: A baked bread pocket stuffed with seasoned beef, cabbage and onion — Nebraska's Volga German heritage in dough.
history: The Runza is a yeast-dough bread pocket filled with seasoned beef, cabbage and onion, brought to Nebraska by Volga Germans — ethnic Germans who had emigrated to Russia before coming to America in the 1870s. Nebraska has its own Runza fast-food chain, unique to the state.

steps.instruction: Dissolve yeast in warm milk with sugar. Add butter and eggs. Mix in flour and salt to form a dough. Knead until smooth. Let rise 1 hour.
Brown ground beef with onion. Add cabbage and cook until wilted. Season well. Cool.
Divide dough into 8 portions. Roll each into a rectangle about 15x20cm.
Place a generous scoop of filling in the centre of each. Fold dough over and pinch edges firmly closed.
Place seam-side down on a baking sheet. Bake at 190C for 20-25 minutes until golden.

name: Nevada Frito Pie
short_description: Chilli ladled over corn chips and blanketed in cheese — Nevada's irresistible casserole.
history: Nevada's gaming culture attracts people from everywhere, and Frito Pie — chilli served over corn chips, topped with melted cheese — has become a beloved comfort food throughout the state. It is the ultimate crowd-pleasing dish.

steps.instruction: Brown beef with onion and garlic. Add tomatoes, beans and spices. Simmer 20 minutes.
Preheat oven to 180C. Spread corn chips in the base of a casserole dish.
Pour chilli over the chips. Top with grated cheese.
Bake for 20 minutes until cheese is melted. Serve with sour cream and jalapeños.

name: New Hampshire Clam Chowder
short_description: Thick, creamy New England clam chowder with salt pork, potato and sweet Atlantic clams.
history: New Hampshire's Atlantic coastline is rich in seafood. New England Clam Chowder is arguably America's most famous soup — a rich, creamy potage of clams, potato and salt pork that sustained coastal fishing communities for centuries.

ingredients.item: fresh clams (or 2 tins clam meat with juice)
salt pork or streaky bacon, diced

steps.instruction: Fry salt pork until crispy. Remove and set aside. Sauté onion in the rendered fat.
Add potatoes, clam juice and bay leaf. Simmer until potatoes are just tender, about 12 minutes.
Add clam meat and simmer 5 minutes. Reduce heat to very low.
Stir in milk and cream. Heat gently without boiling. Season with salt and white pepper.
Serve in warm bowls topped with crispy pork, parsley and oyster crackers.

name: New Jersey Hot Dog with Diner Salad
short_description: A griddled all-beef hot dog in a toasted roll, served alongside a classic New Jersey diner salad.
history: New Jersey is the Diner Capital of America — there are more diners per square mile here than anywhere else on earth. The hot dog, brought by German immigrants, became a New Jersey institution. This recipe pairs it with the Italian-dressed salad served at every Jersey diner.

steps.instruction: Griddle or grill hot dogs over medium-high heat until slightly caramelized on all sides.
Toast the rolls. Place hot dogs in rolls with mustard, ketchup and relish.
Combine lettuce, tomatoes, cucumber, onion and olives in a bowl.
Toss with Italian dressing and top with Parmesan. Serve alongside the hot dogs.

name: New Mexico Green Chili
short_description: Braised pork in a fiery, fragrant sauce of roasted Hatch green chiles — the lifeblood of New Mexico.
history: The Sykora and Bagley families, relatives of Allison Battcock, were stationed at White Sands, New Mexico. New Mexico's green chili is the lifeblood of the state. 'Red or green?' is the official state question. The Hatch Valley produces the world's finest green chiles.

ingredients.item: roasted Hatch green chiles, peeled and chopped (or tinned)

steps.instruction: Brown pork in batches in hot oil. Remove and set aside.
Sauté onion and garlic in the same pan until softened.
Return pork to pot. Add green chiles, stock, cumin and oregano. Bring to a boil.
Reduce heat, cover and simmer 1 to 1.5 hours until pork is very tender. Season.
Serve in bowls with warm tortillas, or ladle over fried eggs, burritos or chips.

name: New York Eggs Benedict
short_description: Poached eggs and Canadian bacon on toasted muffins under a velvety hollandaise — New York's brunch institution.
history: Gabriel and Sylvia Battcock are in New York. The Brevoorts, an old Dutch New York family, were among those who put the bend in Broadway. Eggs Benedict was invented in New York City in the 1890s — either at Delmonico's or the Waldorf Hotel — as the ultimate indulgent brunch.

steps.instruction: Hollandaise: whisk egg yolks and lemon juice in a heatproof bowl over barely simmering water until thick. Slowly drizzle in warm butter, whisking constantly, until emulsified. Season with salt and cayenne.
Bring a wide pan of water to a gentle simmer. Add vinegar. Crack eggs into cups and slide in gently. Poach 3-4 minutes.
Warm Canadian bacon in a pan.
Assemble: toasted muffin half, then bacon, then poached egg. Spoon hollandaise generously over each. Serve immediately.

name: North Carolina Livermush
short_description: A sliceable pork liver and cornmeal loaf, fried until crispy — North Carolina Piedmont's most distinctive food.
history: Livermush is found almost exclusively in the Piedmont region of North Carolina, brought by German settlers to the Carolina foothills. A pork liver and cornmeal loaf, it is sliced and fried for breakfast or eaten in sandwiches with mustard — a unique Carolinian tradition.

steps.instruction: Simmer liver and pork shoulder in 1 litre of water until cooked through, about 45 minutes. Reserve the broth.
Mince the cooked meat very finely in a food processor.
Bring 750ml of the cooking broth to a boil. Whisk in cornmeal and cook until very thick. Stir in minced meat and all seasonings.
Pour into greased loaf pans and refrigerate overnight until firm.
Slice about 1cm thick and fry in a lightly oiled skillet until browned and crispy on each side.

name: North Dakota Knoepla Soup
short_description: Thick, creamy dumpling soup brought by German-Russian immigrants — the taste of the Dakota plains.
history: North Dakota has the highest percentage of German-Russian heritage of any US state. Knoepla (also Knoephla) is a thick dumpling soup brought by German immigrants from Russia to the Dakota plains in the 1870s. It is North Dakota's most beloved and distinctive dish.

steps.instruction: Mix flour, salt and baking powder. Beat milk and egg together, then combine to make a soft, slightly sticky dough.
Sauté onion in butter until soft. Add stock and potato. Bring to a boil.
Pinch or snip the dough into small pieces (about 2cm) and drop directly into the simmering soup.
Cook for 10-15 minutes until dumplings are cooked through and fluffy.
Stir in cream. Season and simmer gently for 5 minutes. Serve in deep bowls.

name: Ohio Cincinnati Chili
short_description: A uniquely spiced vegetarian Cincinnati chili with cinnamon, allspice and cocoa — served over spaghetti with cheese.
history: Cincinnati Chili was invented in 1922 by Macedonian immigrants Tom and John Kiradjieff. Unlike Texas chili, it contains warm spices like cinnamon, allspice and chocolate — served over spaghetti as a 'two-way', or with beans and cheese as a 'five-way'. Solomon Spalding, a possible ancestor, was from Ohio.

ingredients.item: spaghetti, grated cheddar and oyster crackers

steps.instruction: Sauté onion and garlic in oil until soft.
Add tomatoes, beans, all spices, cocoa and vinegar. Stir well.
Simmer uncovered on low heat for 45 minutes, stirring occasionally.
Cook spaghetti. Serve chili over spaghetti, piled high with grated cheddar. Add oyster crackers on the side.

name: Oklahoma Fried Okra with Cowboy Caviar
short_description: Crispy cornmeal-fried okra served with a zesty black-eyed pea and corn salsa.
history: Oklahoma was settled in the Land Rush of 1889. Okra arrived with enslaved Africans and became a staple of Southern and Southwestern cooking. Cowboy Caviar — a black-eyed pea and corn salsa — is a party dip across the Sooner State.

steps.instruction: Mix cornmeal, flour and salt. Toss okra slices in the mixture to coat.
Fry in hot oil for 2-3 minutes until golden and crispy. Drain on paper towels.
Combine all Cowboy Caviar ingredients. Toss well and refrigerate for 30 minutes.
Serve fried okra alongside the Cowboy Caviar with tortilla chips.

name: Oregon Johnny Cakes
short_description: Simple golden cornmeal griddle cakes — the trail bread that fuelled pioneers on the Oregon Trail.
history: Benjamin Mathews studied at the University of Oregon in Portland and joined the Communist Party in 1939. Oregon's pioneer heritage is captured in johnnycakes — cornmeal flatbreads that sustained settlers on the Oregon Trail. The name likely derives from 'journey cake'.

steps.instruction: Mix cornmeal, salt and sugar. Stir in boiling water and let stand for 5 minutes.
Stir in melted butter and milk to make a thick, pourable batter.
Heat a well-oiled griddle over medium heat.
Drop spoonfuls of batter onto the griddle. Cook for 3-4 minutes per side until golden and crispy at the edges.
Serve hot with butter and maple syrup, or with savoury toppings like sour cream and chives.

name: Pennsylvania Stromboli
short_description: A rolled pizza-bread stuffed with salami and mozzarella, baked golden — invented in Essington, Pennsylvania in 1950.
history: Many family ancestors passed through Pennsylvania — the Ensmingers, Albrights and Garmans all had Pennsylvania roots. The Stromboli was invented by Nazzareno Romano at Romano's Italian Restaurant in Essington in 1950. Named after the Italian volcano, it is a rolled pizza-bread stuffed with meat and cheese.

steps.instruction: Preheat oven to 200C. Roll dough into a rectangle about 30x40cm on a floured surface.
Spread marinara over the dough leaving a 2cm border. Layer with salami, then mozzarella.
Sprinkle with oregano and garlic powder.
Roll up tightly from the long side. Pinch seam firmly closed and tuck ends under.
Brush all over with beaten egg. Bake 25-30 minutes until deep golden. Rest 5 minutes before slicing.

name: Puerto Rico Bacalaitos
short_description: Crispy Puerto Rican salt cod fritters sold at every street corner and beach festival on the island.
history: Puerto Rico's cuisine blends Taíno, Spanish, African and American influences. Bacalaitos are crispy salt cod fritters — a beloved street snack at Puerto Rican festivals and beach cookouts. The name comes from bacalao, the salt cod central to Caribbean and Iberian cooking.

ingredients.item: salted cod (bacalao), soaked overnight and drained

steps.instruction: Simmer soaked cod for 15 minutes. Drain, cool and shred into small flakes.
Whisk flour and baking powder with water to make a batter. Stir in shredded cod, garlic, coriander and achiote.
Rest batter for 10 minutes.
Drop spoonfuls into hot oil (180C) and fry until golden and crispy, about 2-3 minutes per side.
Drain and serve immediately with hot sauce and cold beer.

name: Rhode Island Clam Cakes
short_description: Deep-fried clam fritters — Rhode Island's summer institution at every clambake and seafood shack.
history: Rhode Island's culinary identity is defined by Narragansett Bay. Clam cakes are not cakes but deep-fried clam fritters — a Rhode Island summer tradition served at clambakes alongside chowder throughout the state.

steps.instruction: Drain clams and reserve the juice.
Mix flour, baking powder, salt and pepper. Beat in egg and enough clam juice to make a thick batter.
Fold in the minced clams.
Drop spoonfuls into hot oil at 180C. Fry 3-4 minutes until golden and puffed.
Drain on paper towels and serve hot with clam chowder.

name: American Samoa Fiafia Eleni
short_description: Fish gently simmered in coconut cream with ginger and onion — a taste of the South Pacific.
history: American Samoa is the southernmost US territory, a group of islands in the South Pacific. The Samoan people have a rich food tradition rooted in fresh fish and coconut. Fiafia means celebration in Samoan — this fragrant coconut fish dish is served at feasts and family gatherings.

steps.instruction: Season fish with salt and lime juice.
Place fish in a shallow pan. Layer sliced onion, ginger and garlic over the fish.
Pour coconut cream over everything.
Cook over gentle heat for 15-20 minutes until fish is just cooked through and flaking.
Serve over steamed taro or rice, garnished with coriander and spring onions.

name: South Carolina Shrimp and Grits
short_description: Lowcountry Gullah shrimp over creamy cheddar grits with bacon — from humble origins to Southern icon.
history: Shrimp and grits originated with the Gullah Geechee people of the South Carolina Lowcountry — enslaved Africans who preserved their cultural identity through food. What began as a simple breakfast for Charleston fishermen became one of the most celebrated dishes in American Southern cuisine.

steps.instruction: Cook grits in salted water or stock for 20-25 minutes, stirring frequently. Stir in cheese and 2 tablespoons butter. Season and keep warm.
Fry bacon until crispy. Remove and set aside. Sauté onion and green pepper in the bacon fat until soft.
Add garlic and shrimp. Cook 2-3 minutes until shrimp are pink and just cooked through.
Add lemon juice and Tabasco. Return bacon to the pan.
Serve shrimp mixture over bowls of cheesy grits.

name: South Dakota Chislic
short_description: Deep-fried cubes of seasoned lamb on skewers with saltines — a uniquely South Dakotan tradition from Russian Mennonites.
history: Chislic was brought to South Dakota by Russian Mennonite immigrants from the Black Sea region in the 1870s. The name comes from the Russian shashlik. It is made from cubed lamb, deep-fried and served with saltine crackers — a tradition found almost exclusively in South Dakota.

steps.instruction: Season lamb cubes with garlic salt, pepper and onion powder. Refrigerate for at least 1 hour.
Heat oil to 180C in a deep pan.
Fry lamb in batches for 3-4 minutes until browned on the outside but still slightly pink inside.
Drain on paper towels. Season with extra garlic salt.
Serve on skewers or in a bowl with saltine crackers and hot sauce.

name: Nashville Hot Chicken
short_description: Fiery cayenne-coated fried chicken served on white bread with pickles — Nashville's most famous creation.
history: Nashville Hot Chicken was invented in the 1930s by Thornton Prince, whose girlfriend tried to punish him by dousing his fried chicken in hot pepper. The punishment became a pleasure, and Prince's Hot Chicken Shack became a Nashville institution. The Battcock family photographed across Tennessee.

ingredients.item: lard or oil (for the hot paste)

steps.instruction: Soak chicken in buttermilk for at least 4 hours, preferably overnight.
Mix flour with cayenne, paprika and garlic powder. Coat chicken pieces thoroughly.
Deep fry in hot oil (175C) for 12-15 minutes until cooked through and deeply golden.
Melt lard and stir in cayenne, brown sugar and paprika to make a hot paste. Brush generously over hot chicken.
Serve on white bread with pickle slices underneath and on top.

name: Tennessee Elvis Cake
short_description: A banana pound cake layered with peanut butter cream cheese frosting — honouring The King's legendary appetite.
history: Elvis Presley was born in Tupelo, Mississippi but made Memphis, Tennessee his home at Graceland. His legendary food preferences — peanut butter, banana and bacon — inspired this indulgent cake, combining his favourite flavours in a rich layered pound cake.

steps.instruction: Preheat oven to 180C. Cream butter and sugar until light and fluffy. Beat in eggs one at a time, then vanilla.
Mix dry ingredients. Add alternately with sour cream to the butter mixture. Fold in mashed banana.
Pour into two greased and floured 23cm round tins. Bake 30-35 minutes until a skewer comes out clean.
Beat cream cheese and peanut butter until smooth. Add icing sugar and milk. Beat until creamy.
Cool cakes completely. Fill and frost with peanut butter frosting.

name: Texas Bowl of Red
short_description: True Texas chili — beef and dried chiles only, no beans, no tomatoes. The state dish of Texas.
history: Texas Chili, or Bowl of Red, is the official state dish of Texas and the subject of fierce cook-off competition. It originated with cattle drive chuck wagons in the 1850s. In true Texas chili there are no beans, no tomatoes — just beef and chillies. Purists are emphatic on this point.

steps.instruction: Toast dried chiles in a dry pan for 1 minute, then soak in hot water for 20 minutes. Blend to a smooth paste.
Brown beef in batches in hot fat, seasoning with salt. Remove and set aside.
Sauté onion and garlic. Add chile paste and cook 3 minutes.
Return beef to pot. Add stock, cumin and oregano. Simmer covered for 2-3 hours until beef is fork-tender.
Uncover for the last 30 minutes to reduce. Serve with cornbread and raw diced onion.

name: Utah Funeral Potatoes
short_description: The Mormon comfort casserole — creamy hash browns in soup and sour cream with a buttered cornflake crust.
history: Funeral Potatoes are synonymous with Utah's Mormon culture — the casserole that Relief Society sisters bring to bereaved families. The dish became so emblematic of Utah that the 2002 Salt Lake City Winter Olympics featured it on a commemorative pin. Comforting, filling and always welcome.

steps.instruction: Preheat oven to 180C. Combine hash browns, soup, sour cream, cheese, half the butter and onion. Season.
Spread into a greased 23x33cm baking dish.
Toss crushed cornflakes with remaining melted butter. Spread over the top.
Bake for 45-50 minutes until golden, bubbling and the topping is crispy.

name: Vermont Yankee Beans
short_description: Baked beans sweetened with Vermont maple syrup — a New England tradition as old as the republic.
history: Ebenezer Hadley, the earliest American ancestor of the Mathews family through Allison, fought in the Revolutionary War in Vermont and survived an Indian attack. Vermont pure maple syrup is the finest in America, and Yankee Beans — maple-sweetened baked beans — are a New England staple.

ingredients.item: dried navy or pinto beans, soaked overnight
salt pork or streaky bacon, diced

steps.instruction: Drain soaked beans. Parboil for 10 minutes and drain again.
Layer salt pork and onion at the bottom of a Dutch oven or bean pot.
Mix maple syrup, sugar, mustard, salt and pepper with 500ml water.
Add beans and pour maple mixture over. Add just enough water to cover.
Cover and bake at 150C for 6-8 hours, stirring occasionally and adding water as needed. Uncover for the last hour.

name: Virginia Peanut Pie
short_description: A rich peanut custard pie using Virginia's finest peanuts — a Southern cousin to pecan pie.
history: Edward Spalding, the earliest family ancestor to arrive in America, landed in Virginia before the Pilgrim Fathers reached Plymouth. David Reed took George Washington to court over a land dispute in Virginia. Peanuts have been grown in the Virginia Tidewater since the 17th century.

steps.instruction: Preheat oven to 180C. Scatter peanuts evenly in the unbaked pie shell.
Whisk together eggs, corn syrup, sugar, butter, vanilla and salt.
Pour custard mixture over the peanuts.
Bake for 50-60 minutes until filling is set with only a slight wobble in the centre.
Cool completely before slicing. Serve with whipped cream.

name: Washington Apple Pie
short_description: A double-crust apple pie made with Washington's finest Honeycrisp and Fuji apples.
history: Allison Battcock and her family have photographs across Washington State. Washington produces 60% of all US apples — Honeycrisp, Fuji, Pink Lady and Granny Smith. The state's apples make for an extraordinary pie filling — fragrant, tart and perfectly balanced.

ingredients.item: Washington apples (mixed varieties), peeled and sliced

steps.instruction: Preheat oven to 220C. Toss sliced apples with sugar, flour, cinnamon, nutmeg and lemon juice.
Line a pie dish with pastry and fill with apple mixture. Dot with butter.
Add pastry lid and crimp edges firmly. Cut steam vents and brush with egg wash.
Bake at 220C for 20 minutes, then reduce to 190C and bake a further 40 minutes until golden.
Cool for at least 2 hours before slicing. Serve with vanilla ice cream.

name: West Virginia Chow Chow
short_description: A tangy Appalachian pickled vegetable relish of cabbage, green tomato and pepper — served with beans and cornbread.
history: West Virginia's Appalachian mountain culture developed its own rich food traditions. Chow Chow is the great Appalachian relish — a tangy, sweet-sour preserved vegetable condiment made to use up late-season garden surplus, served with pinto beans, cornbread and pork throughout the mountain state.

steps.instruction: Combine all chopped vegetables, sprinkle with salt and toss. Let sit overnight, then rinse thoroughly and drain.
Bring vinegar, sugar and spices to a boil in a large pot.
Add drained vegetables. Simmer for 10 minutes.
Pack into sterilised jars and seal. Process in a boiling water bath for 10 minutes.
Rest 2 weeks before opening. Serve cold alongside pinto beans and cornbread.

name: Wisconsin Brats and Beer Chowder
short_description: A hearty Wisconsin chowder of bratwurst and potato in a creamy, beer-enriched broth.
history: Wisconsin has the highest per-capita German heritage of any US state. Bratwurst arrived with immigrants in the 19th century and became the state's favourite food. Cooking brats in beer is a Wisconsin summer tradition. This chowder combines both into one warming bowl.

steps.instruction: Brown brat slices in butter. Remove and set aside.
Sauté onion and garlic in the same pan. Add beer and scrape up any browned bits.
Add stock and potatoes. Simmer until potatoes are tender, about 15 minutes.
Return brats to pot. Stir in cream and cheese until melted. Season with salt, pepper and a spoonful of mustard.
Serve in deep bowls with crusty bread.

name: Wyoming White Chili
short_description: A warming white chili of chicken and cannellini beans with green chiles and cumin — the cowboy's winter bowl.
history: Wyoming is America's least populated state — wide open ranchland, mountains and frontier spirit. White chili — made with chicken and white beans instead of beef and red — has become a Wyoming cowboy cook classic, perfect for warming up after long days on the range.

steps.instruction: Brown chicken in oil and set aside. Sauté onion and garlic until soft.
Add green chiles, cumin and oregano. Cook 2 minutes.
Return chicken. Add beans and stock. Bring to a boil.
Simmer for 30 minutes. Stir in sour cream and cream. Heat gently — do not boil.
Season and serve topped with grated cheese alongside cornbread.

name: Wyoming Cowboy Cookies
short_description: Oversized, chunky cookies loaded with oats, chocolate chips, coconut and pecans — the cowboy trail's sustaining snack.
history: Cowboy Cookies originated on Wyoming cattle drives — a substantial, energy-dense treat that could sustain a ranch hand through a full day of hard work. These oversized cookies became so beloved that a recipe attributed to Laura Bush won the 2000 Presidential Cookie Bake-Off.

steps.instruction: Preheat oven to 180C. Cream butter and both sugars until light and fluffy. Beat in eggs and vanilla.
Mix together flour, baking soda, baking powder and salt. Stir into the butter mixture.
Fold in oats, chocolate chips, coconut and pecans.
Drop generous rounded spoonfuls onto lined baking sheets, leaving space to spread.
Bake for 12-14 minutes until edges are golden but centres are still slightly soft. Cool on a wire rack.
